Name:  ___                    Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: MEDICINE
 
Allergies: 
No Allergies/ADRs on File
 
Attending: ___
 
___ Complaint:
Dyspnea

 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Evaluation for airway stent

 
History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o male with a history of epigoltic squamos 
cell carcinoma diagnosed ___ ___ s/p chemo/radiation 
completed ___ ___, adenocarcinoma of stage IV left lung 
diagnosed ___ s/p cycle 3 of 5 of chemo completed on 
___ who was found to have new tracheal tumor at the level 
of the carina causing worsening dyspnea who is transferred to 
___ for evaluation of airway stent. 

The patient originally presented on ___ to ___ 
with progressive dyspnea and worsening cough. He reports 
worsening SOB x 1 month and intermittent episodes of cough 
productive of clear to yellow sputum. Denies hemoptysis. He was 
found to have sepesis secondary to bilateral pneumonia and 
sepsis. The patient was started on vanc/cefepime/azithromycin. 
He decompensated with hypotension and was transferred to ICU and 
diflucan was added. With Abx and fluid resusitation he improved. 
Course complicated by thrombocytopenia and was diagnosed with 
HIT with positive antibody assay. Diflucan d/c prior to transfer 
to ___. Heparin was d/c and plts improved. The patient 
then developed acute on chronic renal failure with a creatinine 
of 4.4 (baseline 1.3-1.5) and was transferred on hospital day ___ 
(___) to ___ for possibility of dialysis. 

While at ___ patient has been afebrile and blood 
cultures negative. Per report OSH cultures showed blood culture 
of strep viridans and MSSA ___ sputum. On day ___ of vanc/cefepime 
on day of transfer. The patient had UOP of 2L/day and dialysis 
was not required per renal team. While at ___ patient 
had worsening dyspnea despite treatment of pneumonia. 
Additionally, patient had coughing fits with hemoptysis. Concern 
for PE and was started on agatroban. He then had a low 
probability V/Q scan and negative bilateral lower extremity 
ultrasounds and argatroban was stopped. CT chest revealed 
continued bibasilar opacities with new tumor causing airway 
narrowing above carina. The patient was placed on 10L highflow 
NC however continued to experience respiratory distress 
requiring a course of BiPAP and then weaned to again to 10L 
highflow NC.  On exertion or movement the patient desats to 
89-90%. HR has been stable ___ ___, BPs 130s/70s, RR ___. Per 
report the patient understands stenting would be palliatve 
measure and is transferred for stent. The patient was on 10L 
highflow NC with saturations >93% on transfer.

On arrival to the MICU, the patient reports that he feels ok. He 
states that his SOB is about the same throughout his course. He 
continues to have a cough productive of clear to yellow sputum. 
He complains of pain ___ his right shoulder and over his 
buttocks. Reports right shoulder pain is chronic from rotator 
cuff problem and developed buttock pain ___ hospital from lying 
___ bed.
 
Past Medical History:
1. Squamos cell carcinoma of epiglottis, treated with 
neoadjuvant chemo and radiation, completed radiation therapy ___ 
___ per report. Followed by ___ Oncology.
2. Stage IV Adenocarcinoma of left lung - diagnosed concurrently 
with ___ above ___ ___. Per family has recieved 3 cycles of 
chemotherapy.
3. CKD stage III (baseline 1.3-1.5)
4. COPD
5. Tobacco Abuse
6. Hypertension
7. Hyperlipidemia
8. GERD
9. History of microscopic hematuria with known uric acid 
nephrolithiasis
10. Right Rotator cuff tear

CXR ___: Tracheal and esophageal stents are unchanged ___ their 
respective positions.  Severe pneumonia ___ both lower lobes has 
not changed appreciably.  Enlarged heart and distortion of the 
mediastinal contour by extensive adenopathy are stable.  Pleural 
effusions are presumed, but not large.  There is no 
pneumothorax.  Extensive asbestos-related pleural calcification 
noted.  Right PIC line passes as far as the mid SVC.  ET tube ___ 
standard placement. 
 
Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o male with a history of epiglottic squamous 
cell carcinoma diagnosed ___ ___ s/p chemo/radiation 
completed ___ ___, adenocarcinoma of stage IV left lung 
diagnosed ___ s/p cycle 3 of 5 of chemo completed on 
___ who was found to have new tracheal tumor at the level 
of the carina causing worsening dyspnea who was transferred to 
___ for evaluation of airway stent now s/p esophageal and 
tracheal stent placement. 

# Stage IV Lung Adenocarcinoma and epiglottic squamous cell 
carcinoma: s/p chemo/radiation completed ___ ___ for epiglotic 
SCC. s/p cycle 3 of 5 of chemo completed on ___ for lung 
adenocarcinoma (documentation of carboplatin regimen). Now with 
new tracheal tumor and critical tracheal narrowing.
- Tracheal stent placed by IP ___
- GI placed esophageal stent ___

# Goals of Care: Multiple discussions held with family. On 
admission, family wanted to pursue agressive interventions where 
able to prolong/improve patient's life. Stent placed as above 
without significant improvement ___ symptoms. Respiratory status 
continued to decline. Further discussions held with family and 
with primary oncologist. Family is aware of dire circumstances 
and purely palliative options. Would like patient DNR/DNI but 
not CMO. Transfer back to ___ at the request of the 
family so that they can be closer and will begin to discuss 
hospice. 
Symptom Control: 
- IV dilaudid for pain
- Ativan for anxiety
- Nebulizers
 
# Pneumonia: patient diagnosed with pneumonia on ___ at ___ 
___. Reported as MSSA ___ sputum from ___ 
___. Bronchoscopy here revealed purulent secretions ___ the 
airways. 
- Continue vanc/cefepime/flagyl for post-obstructive PNA given 
purulence found during procedure and unimproved lung exam/CXR. 
Plan for 14 day course unless GOC change.

# Hypoxia/Dyspnea: Pt found to have new tracheal tumor at level 
of carina at ___ likely the major contributing 
factor to dyspnea and hypoxia. Currently also undergoing 
treatment for pna. V/Q scan at OSH showed low probability of PE. 
Pt also with h/o COPD and started on steroids at OSH and 
standing nebs for ?COPD exacerbation.
- pt now extubated and on NRB
- Treat pna as above
- Q6 hour nebs
- Treat COPD as below

# Acute on chronic renal failure: Per report baseline Cr 
1.2-1.5. Patient originally transferred to ___ for 
concern for need of dialysis, however no dialysis needed. 
Thought to be secondary to hypotension induced ATN ___ setting of 
infection with possible contribution from carboplatin and 
vancomycin. Renal at ___ consulted  no urgent need for 
dialysis and likely would not be ___ line with goals of care.

# Strep Viridans Bacteremia: Blood culture from ___ 
___ showed Strep viridans ___ ___ from ___. No e/o 
endocarditis and it does not seem TEE would be ___ line with ___. 


# COPD: Not on home O2. On steroids for unclear reason started 
at ___. Presently tapering steroids, now on 
10mg daily for two additional days. Holding tiotropium while on 
short acting anticholinergic. 

# HIT: Diagnosed at ___. Avoiding heparin 
products.  

# HTN: Continued home metoprolol. Held diovan given ___. 

Transitional Issues:
-Please consider palliative care consult

Name:  ___                   Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   F
 
Service: NEUROLOGY
 
Allergies: 
Bactrim / Prilosec / Protonix
 
Attending: ___
 
Chief Complaint:
right sided visual field loss
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
none

 
History of Present Illness:
The patient is a ___ year old woman who presents with right
sided vision loss. She was normal one night prior to
presentation. She awoke around ___ feeling okay, and she arose
from bed at 0930. She did not notice any problems at this time,
but when she sat down to work on a computer at ___, she noted
that she had some difficulty seeing the right sided of the
screen. The area of visual disturbance was not entirely
well-demarcated, but it was present with either eye closed. She
had a mild head "pressure" sensation during this time but no
other symptoms. She took some Ibuprofen and the head pressure
sensation resolved. She asked her husband to take a look at her,
and he did not notice any overt external abnormalities. She went
down to the kitchen and accidentally knocked over a glass on the
table or counter to her right and had difficulty finding objects
on that side below her line of vision. This prompted an urgent
visit to her primary care physician who sent her to the ED for
further evaluation. Her symptoms had not changed or evolved
during the course prior to her arrival.

On neurologic review of systems, the patient denies major
headache, lightheadedness, or confusion. 
Denies difficulty with producing or comprehending speech.
Endorses loss of vision. Denies blurred vision, diplopia,
vertigo, tinnitus, hearing difficulty, dysarthria, or dysphagia.
Denies muscle weakness.
Denies loss of sensation.
Denies bowel or bladder incontinence or retention.
Denies difficulty with gait.

On general review of systems, the patient denies fevers, rigors,
night sweats, or noticeable weight loss.
Denies chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, or cough.
Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, or abdominal
pain.
Denies dysuria or hematuria.
Denies myalgias, arthralgias, or rash.

 
Past Medical History:
Migraines
HTN
Low back pain
GERD, Duodenal ulcer (remote)
Vitamin D Deficiency
Anxiety

 
Social History:
___
Family History:
No stroke. MI (Father, at advanced age). No
migraines.

 
Physical Exam:
Physical Exam on Admission:

VS T: 97.6 HR: 66 BP: 154/75 RR: 18 SaO2: 100% RA 
General: NAD, seated in bed comfortably, obese
Head: NC/AT, no conjunctival icterus, no oropharyngeal lesions
Neck: Supple, no nuchal rigidity, no 
carotid/subclavian/vertebral
bruits
Cardiovascular: RRR, no M/R/G
Pulmonary: Equal air entry bilaterally, no crackles or wheezes
Abdomen: Soft, NT, ND, +BS, no guarding
Extremities: Warm, no edema, palpable radial/dorsalis pedis
pulses
Skin: No rashes or lesions

___ Stroke Scale - Total [1]
1a. Level of Consciousness - 0
1b. LOC Questions - 0
1c. LOC Commands - 0
2. Best Gaze - 0
3. Visual Fields - 1
4. Facial Palsy - 0
5a. Motor arm, left - 0
5b. Motor arm, right - 0
6a. Motor leg, left - 0
6b. Motor leg, right - 0
7. Limb Ataxia - 0
8. Sensory - 0
9. Language - 0
10. Dysarthria - 0
11. Extinction and Neglect - 0

Neurologic Examination:

- Mental Status - Awake, alert, oriented x 3. Attention to
examiner easily attained and maintained. Recalls a coherent
history. Structure of speech demonstrates fluency with full
sentences, intact repetition, and intact verbal comprehension.
Content of speech demonstrates intact naming (high and low

TCD Report

Impression: Abnormal study. No microembolic signals were seen
after agitated saline injection during normal respiration. After
the second agitated saline injection with Valsalva manuever, six
microemboli signals were seen. After the third agitated saline
injection, ___ microemboli signals were seen. By the
___ Scale criteria, this was a Grade II right to

septum / pulmonary shunt.

CT head w/o contrast

 
There is no acute intracranial hemorrhage, edema, mass effect, 
or evidence of an acute large vascular territorial infarction.  
The ventricles and sulci are normal in size and configuration.  
There are scattered calcifications in the cavernous and 
supraclinoid internal carotid arteries.  There is no fracture.  
There are partially imaged mucous retention cysts in the 
maxillary sinuses.   
  
IMPRESSION: 
No acute hemorrhage. No evidence of acute major vascular 
territory infarction, but MRI would be more sensitive for an 
acute infarction, if clinically warranted. 
  
MRI head and MRA head/neck

FINDINGS:  There is an acute infarction in the left inferior 
occipital lobe.  There is no hydrocephalus.  Flow voids are 
maintained.  MRA of the Circle of ___ demonstrates poor 
filling of the right distal PCA with a focal stenosis in the 
left P2 segment as well.  Basilar artery is patent.  No 
high-grade stenosis in the anterior circulation.  No aneurysm 
within limits of examination. 
MRA of the neck demonstrates patency of the bilateral carotid 
and vertebral arteries.  No high-grade stenosis is seen.  The 
right vertebral artery is smaller than the left. 
  
IMPRESSION: 
Left inferior occipital lobe acute infarction.  Atherosclerotic 
disease in bilateral distal PCA. 

Bilateral LENIs

No evidence of DVT in the right or left lower extremities.

TTE

The left atrium is elongated. A patent foramen ovale is present. 
Left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size and regional/global 
systolic function are normal (LVEF >55%). Right ventricular 
chamber size and free wall motion are normal. The diameters of 
aorta at the sinus, ascending and arch levels are normal. The 
aortic valve leaflets (3) appear structurally normal with good 
leaflet excursion and no aortic stenosis or aortic 
regurgitation. The mitral valve appears structurally normal with 
trivial mitral regurgitation. The estimated pulmonary artery 
systolic pressure is normal. There is no pericardial effusion. 

IMPRESSION: Patent foramen ovale. Normal global and regional 
biventricular systolic function.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ is a ___ h/o migraines with aura, HTN, obesity, 
sedentary lifestyle, and anxiety p/w bilateral right sided 
visual field loss, concerning for an acute ischemic stroke. 

# Neuro: The time of onset of symptoms was not clear as she 
thinks she may have awoken with the symptoms at 0845 but first 
noticed the symptoms while working on a computer at ___. 
Accordingly, her last seen well time was the night prior to 
admission. She described difficulty with vision in both the 
right upper and right lower quandrants with either eye covered; 
at presentation as a Code Stroke she had a congruent right upper 
quadrantanopia without other deficits. While she had a mild head 
pressure sensation alongside this event, this headache was not 
similar to her prior migraines and this event had no preceding 
visual aura (prior typical wavy visual disturbance). Initially, 
thought it was most likely that she had an embolic acute 
ischemic stroke although migraine variants of the elderly is 
also a possibility. MRI brain showed left occipital infarct.  No 
afib on telemetry. TCD with agitated saline-positive for HITs, 
thus, concern for PFO.  Had TTE which confirmed presence of PFO. 
 Bilaeral LENIs as well as MRV pelvis neg for DVT which was 
reassuring.  
Checked stroke risk factors: HbA1c 5.5, LDL 123. Start Aspirin 
325 mg daily and atorvastatin 40mg daily for stroke prevention. 
She will have hypercoag work up as an outpatient for Factor V 
leiden, protein c/s, anti thrombin III, lupus anticoagulant 
antibody, anti cardiolipin. On discharge, she still had right 
upper quadrantanopia.  Given mild visual deficit, felt that 
patient was safe to drive.  She is aware of the deficit and 
understood the importance of making sure she explores her 
environment when driving. She will follow up in stroke clinic.

# Cardiac: Monitored on telemetry, no aberrant rhythms noted as 
above.  

Transitions of Care:
- hypercoag work up ordred as above and results will be faxed to 
Dr. ___
- ___ f/u in stroke clinic as above
- find read MRV pelvis pending at time of discharge
- should have pancreatic cysts followed up in ___ year as an 
outpatient

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ___,

You were admitted to the hospital with decreased vision in your 
right eye.  An MRI of your brain showed that you had a stroke. 
We checked your labs for stroke risk factors and saw that your 
cholesterol was elevated, so we started you on atorvastatin to 
treat it.  You also have a patent foramen ovale (a small 
connection between 2 chambers of your heart) so we were 
concerned that you may have had a blood clot in a vein which 
traveled through this connection to your brain.  We checked you 
for venous blood clots and you DID NOT have any, which was 
reassuring.  So, for a blood thinner, we started you on Aspirin 
daily.  We would like you to have a number of blood tests 
checked for any abnormalities that could predispose you to 
strokes.  A prescription is included.

In terms of driving on discharge, we feel it is safe for you to 
drive.  Your visual deficit is very minor so please continue to 
be aware of it and be careful to fully explore your right visual 
field when you are driving.

We have made the following changes to your medications:

as scheduled below.

Name:  ___                    Unit No:   ___

None

 
History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is an ___ year-old male with a history of dementia, HTN, 
and DMII who presented with syncope and fall with headstrike. 

At approximately 1530, pt had episode of syncope. No preceding 
symptoms. Witnessed. Pt fell backwards. Hit back of head. No 
seizure activity. LOC for few seconds. No postictal stage. After 
episode complained of abdominal pain. Experienced another 
episode of syncope 10 minutes later. Syncopal episode occurred 
while lying flat. LOC for few seconds. After the second episode,
patient developed abdominal pain that was alleviated after one 
episode of vomiting. Family members noted otherwise that patient 
has been having fevers and chills, chest pain, shortness of 
breath.

Notably, 3 months ago had epsidoe of presyncope vs syncope and 
fall, admitted to ___ ___. Cardiac w/u was negative, 
including normal TTE on ___. Found on CT head to have 7mm 
temporal lobe lesion, ASA was discontinued. HCTZ was d/c'd as 
thought to have contributed to his fall. 

Of note, per ___ notes has had worsening functional status over 
the last few months in the setting of his wifes illness. Per 
notes, daughter says his wife was the "stabilizing force." Has 
been requiring 24hr supervision over the last few months due to 
history of falls and wandering. He is now dependent for all 
IADLs and most ADLs.

immediately after study.

IMPRESSION: no intracardiac mass or vegetation seen. Normal 
biventricular systolic function.  

 
Brief Hospital Course:
This is a ___ year old nurse with ___ history of hypertension, 
gastric reflux who presents with altered mental status and 
hyponatremia.  
.  
# Altered mental status: Given intial physical exam, concern was 
for seizure activity and confusion secondary to post-ictal 
state. CT of the head without acute finding. Initially, due to 
fever, leucocytosis and question of seizure, the concern was for 
meningitis, though LP not was not impressive and she had no 
menigeal signs. She was started empirically on IV Ceftriaxone 
and Vancomycin for this possibility.  Neurology followed the 
patient. A brain MRI was negative, as was an EEG. Neurology 
signed off, with the final impression being that this probably 
represented a seizure due to hyponatremia and that no 
antiepileptics were necessary.
.  
# Fever: At OSH presented with ever and leukocytosis with left 
shift. Blood cultures were drawn and were positive (2 out of 2) 
for MSSA. One of the two was positive also for Strep mitis. An 
extensive workup for the source of the MSSA bacteremia included 
TTE (negative), multiple blood cultures (negative), UA 
(negative), CXR (negative)and an abdominal CT (negative, athough 
without contrast it is a suboptimal study). ID reccomended a 2 
week (total) course of IV Vanco. Thw ceftriaxone was stopped on 
___ due to lack of an indication. She remained on the IV 
Vanco and continued to spike fevers. A TEE was performed, and 
was also negative, therefore ruling out endocarditis. It is felt 
that the most likely cause of the bacteremia was the tongue 
biting episide with transient bacteremia. The source of the 
fever despite the IV vanco was felt to probably represent C.Diff 
as she developed profuse watery diarrhea near the end of her 
hospitalization. Two stool toxin assays were negative, but the 
clinical suspicion was so high that despite this she was 
discharged home on Flagyl PO for a ___ PICC line 
was placed prior to discharge so that she could finish her 14 
day course of IV Vanco at home.
.  
# Hyponatremia: Initially with sodium of 118, with urine lytes 
c/w hypovolemia.  Improved with hydration.  Subequently urine 
lytes were more c/w SIDAH. She was placed on a fluid restriction 
and her sodium normalized. Nephrology felt that this was  not 
SIADH but more likely ADH secretion in the face of SSRI, ETOH 
intake, and possible water intoxication. She previously also had 
been on HCTZ. They did not feel she needed water restriction, 
and liberalized her fluids. She was discharged home with 
instructions to limit ETOH intake and drink water in moderation, 
when thirsty. Serum sodium will be followed by her PCP. 

#Acute renal failure: pt's creatinine rose to a peak of 2.2 on 
___. Nephrology was consulted and diagnosed ATN, but the 
insult which caused the tubular necrosis was not identified. Her 
serum creatine began to improve without intervention, and was 
1.8 by the time of discharge.

# Guaiac positive stools: No history of hematemesis, 
hematochezia or melena. Remained hemodynamically stable.  ASA 
was held. ? gastritis - PPI was started. ___ need further workup 
as an outpatient (i.e. endoscopy) if GI bleed persists despite 
PPI.
.  
# Transaminitis: No priors to compare. AST > ALT, likely 
secondary to ETOH.  
.  
# Elevated CK: Negative MBI. Likely secondary to immobilization 
and seizure. Peaked at 8000 on ___, then was nearly normal by 
discharge.

.  

 
Medications on Admission:
Protonix 40 daily  
Celexa unknown dose  
Aspirin 81 daily  
Simvstatin unknown dose

Home With Service
 
Facility:
___
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Probable seizure due to hyponatremia
MSSA bacteremia
Hyponatremia - ? SIADH vs side effect of SSRI
Acute renal failure
Antibiotic-related diarrhea (probable C.Diff)

Stable

You were admitted following a possible seizure due to low 
sodium. This may have been caused by your antidepressant, 
excessive fluid intake, or SIADH. You are advised to avoid 
alcoholic beverages, excessive water intake, and the antibiotic 
class known as SSRIs. You also developed a blood infection, the 
cause of which was not identified. You will need to finish the 
two week course of IV antibiotic for this infection. You also 
developed mild kidney failure, which is improving. You also 
developed antibiotic-related diarrhea (probably C.Diff), for 
which you need to finish a 14 day course of oral antibiotics.

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the orthopaedic surgery service and 
was taken to the operating room for above described procedure. 
Please see separately dictated operative report for details. The 
procedure was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the 
procedure well.

Postoperative course was remarkable for the following:

1. Hypoglycemia - Patient had 3 asymptomatic hypoglycemic 
episodes in the morning, blood sugar 34-50s, resolved with oral 
intake. Have held ___ standing insulin without improvement. 
Please encourage bedtime snack.

Otherwise, pain was initially controlled with a IV pain 
medications followed by a transition to oral pain medications on 
POD#1.  The patient received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis.  The 
patient was seen daily by physical therapy.  At the time of 
discharge the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling 
well.  The patient was afebrile with stable vital signs.  The 
patient's pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. The 
operative extremity was neurovascularly intact.

The patient's weight-bearing status is weight bearing as 
tolerated.

Ms. ___ is discharged to rehab in stable condition.
 
Medications on Admission:
Diltiazem, lisinopril, glyburide, albuterol, iron, glucosamine, 
hydrochlorothiazide, Humulin, vitamin E, Caltrate, cod liver 
oil, omega-3, Zantac, cranberry juice, glucosamine/chondroitin 
sulfate, colace, nystatin, triamcinolone, Flexeril, albuterol
 
Discharge Medications:
1. oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: ___ Tablets PO Q4H (every 4 hours) 
as needed for Pain.
Disp:*90 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
2. albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/actuation HFA Aerosol Inhaler Sig: 
One (1) Puff Inhalation Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for SOB or 
wheezing.  
3. furosemide 20 mg Tablet Sig: Three (3) Tablet PO DAILY 
(Daily).  
4. glyburide 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).  
5. lisinopril 10 mg Tablet Sig: Three (3) Tablet PO DAILY 
(Daily).  
6. calcium carbonate 200 mg calcium (500 mg) Tablet, Chewable 
Sig: One (1) Tablet, Chewable PO DAILY (Daily).  
7. cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 400 unit Tablet Sig: One (1) 
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).  
8. ferrous sulfate 300  mg (60 mg iron) Tablet Sig: One (1) 
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).  
9. ranitidine HCl 75 mg Tablet Sig: home dose Tablet PO as at 
home.  
10. Novolog Mix 70-30 100 unit/mL (70-30) Solution Sig: Twenty 
Five (25) units Subcutaneous QAM.  
11. Novolog Mix 70-30 100 unit/mL (70-30) Solution Sig: Eight 
(8) units Subcutaneous QPM: *Have been holding dose as patient 
having hypoglycemic episodes in AM. Encourage bedtime snack*.  
12. docusate sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO twice 
a day: Take while on narcotic pain meds.  
13. senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day as 
needed for constipation.  
14. enoxaparin 40 mg/0.4 mL Syringe Sig: One (1) syringe 
Subcutaneous once a day for 4 weeks: To prevent a blood clot.  

 
Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care
 
Facility:
___
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Right knee osteoarthritis
Hypoglycemia
 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker 
or cane).

 
Discharge Instructions:
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.

2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills.

3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.

4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.

5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.

6. Please keep your wounds clean. You may shower starting five 
(5) days after surgery, but no tub baths or swimming for at 
least four (4) weeks. No dressing is needed if wound continues 
to be non-draining. Any stitches or staples that need to be 
removed will be taken out at your follow-up visit in three (3) 
weeks after your surgery.

7. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment in three (3) weeks.

8. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
medications (NSAIDs such as celebrex, ibuprofen, advil, aleve, 
motrin, etc).

9. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your lovenox for four (4) 
weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  ___ 
STOCKINGS x 6 WEEKS.

10. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed at your follow-up 
visit in three (3) weeks.

11. ___ (once at home): Home ___, dressing changes as 
instructed, and wound checks.

12. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. Mobilize. CPM/ROM as tolerated. No strenuous exercise 
or heavy lifting until follow up appointment.

Physical Therapy:
RLE WBAT
ROM unrestricted
CPM ___ for 2hr sessions
Mobilize frequently

Treatments Frequency:
Dry sterile dressing daily as needed for drainage
Wound checks
Ice and elevation
TEDs
Blood sugar checks QID
*Staples will be removed at follow-up visit in 3 weeks*

History of Present Illness:
___ who presents to the ED with a new rash. She was in her 
normal state of health until ___ when developed whole body 
aches including left sided chest discomfort as well as 
rhinorrhea. She attributed this to a cold or influenza. She took 
OTC cough syrup and ___ seltzer. On ___, she noticed a 
vesicular rash on her left hand that extended up to her wrist 
but she did not look at any other location to see the extent of 
the rash. She had some mild pain at the site on her hand and 
some pruritis on her upper back. Her son noticed the extent of 
the rash yesterday - traveling up her left arm, her upper back

Brief Hospital Course:
Pt was admitted for cardiac surgery evaluation ___ with 
history of known CAD with PCI in the past, presented with LAD 
and RCA disease. Upon admission, he was hemodynamically stable 
and chest pain free at rest.  EKG with some elevations in 
inferior leads consistent with known disease. Trops were 
negative. Heparin drip was continued for coronary artery 
disease. He was scheduled for CABG. He was taken to the 
operating room on ___ and underwent Coronary artery bypass 
graft x4, Maze, ___ ligation with Dr. ___.  
 Please see operative note for full details. Pt tolerated the 
procedure well and was transferred to the CVICU in stable 
condition for recovery and invasive monitoring. Pt was weaned 
from sedation, awoke neurologically intact, and was extubated on 
POD 1. Pt was weaned from vasopressor support. 
Beta blocker was initiated and pt was diuresed toward his 
preoperative weight. Dofetilide was restarted per EP 
recommendations for preop atrial fibrillation, with close 
monitoring of QtC. Pt remained hemodynamically stable and was 
transferred to the telemetry floor for further recovery. Chest 
tubes and pacing wires were removed per cardiac surgery 
protocol. Eliquis was restarted after pacing wires were pulled 
for atrial fibrillation. He was given Motrin for pericarditis. 
Pt was evaluated by the physical therapy service for assistance 
with their strength and mobility. By the time of discharge on 
POD 4 pt was ambulating freely, all wounds were healing with 
Prevena in place, and pain was controlled with oral analgesics. 
Pt was discharged home in good condition with appropriate follow 
up instructions.

Brief Hospital Course:
BRIEF HISTORY:
 
The patient was admitted to the orthopedic surgery service and 
was taken to the operating room for above described procedure. 
Please see separately dictated operative report for details. The 
surgery was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the 
procedure well. Patient received perioperative IV antibiotics.

Postoperative course was remarkable for the following:
POD#1, The patient was triggered for BP 80/42 while lying in 
bed. She reported feeling tired but was otherwise asymptomatic. 
She was bloused with 500cc NS and her gabapentin dose was 
decreased to 100mg TID. 

POD#2, patient did well overnight.  She was given a 250mL NS 
bolus and 12.5mg Albumin IV in the morning. BPs were stable.  Pt 
cleared physical therapy and was cleared for discharge to rehab. 


Otherwise, pain was controlled with a combination of IV and oral 
pain medications.  The patient received Lovenox 40 mg daily for 
DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of POD#1.  The surgical 
dressing was changed on POD#2 and the surgical incision was 
found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal 
drainage. The patient was seen daily by physical therapy. Labs 
were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted 
accordingly. At the time of discharge the patient was tolerating 
a regular diet and feeling well.  The patient was afebrile with 
stable vital signs.  The patient's hematocrit was acceptable and 
pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. The operative 
extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. 

The patient's weight-bearing status is weight bearing as 
tolerated on the operative extremity with posterior precautions. 

 

Ms ___ is discharged to rehab in stable condition.
 
Medications on Admission:
The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete.
1. Albuterol Inhaler 2 PUFF IH Q2H:PRN wheezing 
2. Atenolol 12.5 mg PO DAILY 
3. Levothyroxine Sodium 100 mcg PO DAILY 
4. Sertraline 50 mg PO DAILY 
5. Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus (100/50) 1 INH IH BID 

 
Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care
 
Facility:
___
 
___ Diagnosis:
Left hip osteoarthritis

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker 
or cane).

 
Discharge Instructions:
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.
 
2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 
 
3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.
 
4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.
 
5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.
 
6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment.
 
7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc) until cleared by 
your physician.

8. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your Lovenox 40 mg daily for 
4 weeks to prevent blood clots.
 
9. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed by the visiting 
nurse or rehab facility in two (2) weeks.
 
10. ___ (once at home): Home ___, dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks, and staple removal at two weeks after 
surgery.
 
11. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. Posterior precautions. No strenuous exercise or heavy 
lifting until follow up appointment.  Mobilize frequently.
 
Physical Therapy:
HIPS:
WBAT affected extremity
Posterior hip precautions for 2 months 
Mobilize frequently
Treatments Frequency:
daily dressing changes as needed for drainage
wound checks daily
ice
staple removal and replace with steri-strips at follow up visit

Discharge Instructions:
1. Please return to the emergency department or notify your 
physician if you experience any of the following: severe pain 
not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased 
sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers greater than 101.5, 
shaking chills, increasing redness or drainage from the incision 
site, chest pain, shortness of breath or any other concerns.

2. Please follow up with your primary physician regarding this 
admission and any new medications and refills. 

3. Resume your home medications unless otherwise instructed.

4. You have been given medications for pain control. Please do 
not drive, operate heavy machinery, or drink alcohol while 
taking these medications. As your pain decreases, take fewer 
tablets and increase the time between doses. This medication can 
cause constipation, so you should drink plenty of water daily 
and take a stool softener (such as Colace) as needed to prevent 
this side effect.  Call your surgeons office 3 days before you 
are out of medication so that it can be refilled.  These 
medications cannot be called into your pharmacy and must be 
picked up in the clinic or mailed to your house.  Please allow 
an extra 2 days if you would like your medication mailed to your 
home.

5. You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your 
surgeon.

6. Please call your surgeon's office to schedule or confirm your 
follow-up appointment.

7. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, 
especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice 
directly on the skin. You may wrap the knee with an ace bandage 
for added compression. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal 
anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, 
ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc) until cleared by 
your physician.

8. ANTICOAGULATION: Please continue your Lovenox daily for four 
(4) weeks to help prevent deep vein thrombosis (blood clots).  
If you were taking aspirin prior to your surgery, it is OK to 
continue at your previous dose after the four weeks is 
completed.  

9. WOUND CARE: Please keep your incision clean and dry. It is 
okay to shower five days after surgery but no tub baths, 
swimming, or submerging your incision until after your four (4) 
week checkup. Please place a dry sterile dressing on the wound 
each day if there is drainage, otherwise leave it open to air. 
Check wound regularly for signs of infection such as redness or 
thick yellow drainage. Staples will be removed at your follow-up 
appointment in two weeks.

10. ___ (once at home): Home ___, dressing changes as 
instructed, wound checks.

11. ACTIVITY: Weight bearing as tolerated on the operative 
extremity. Mobilize.  Posterior precautions. No strenuous 
exercise or heavy lifting until follow up appointment.

Brief Hospital Course:
This is a ___ h/o COPD, depression, hypertension, and 
hypothyroidism who presents with a one week history of 
increasing cough and shortness of breath with copious amounts of 
clear to yellow sputum production.

# COPD Exacerbation: Patient most likely had an asthma induced 
COPD exacerbation, while cleaning up around her house. She 
states she was enveloped in a cloud of dust which exacerbated 
her the shortness of breath which she had previously been 
experiencing over the week. Pt. states she started using her 
allergy medication but well after her allergic symptoms started. 
She has an inhaler but states she was not using it. While in the 
emergency department she was placed on oxygen and was still 
satting at 88%. The patient was given nebulizers x3, prednisone 
60 mg PO and had a negative chest xray. She did have a d-Dimer 
of 432. While on the floor Nebulizer treatments were given 
q-6hrs and oxygen was titrated as needed to keep O2 sats between 
90-94%. She started at 3L of oxygen at 94% and was eventually 
titrated down to RA. She was given prednisone to reduce the 
inflammation. She also was given codeine which helped with her 
cough and sleep. She was given fexofenadine to treat the 
allergic component. Once taken off of oxygen she maintained an 
O2 sat of 90-93%. I then checked 2 ambulatory O2 sats which 
ranged from 88-90%. Pt. was comfortable and did not complain of 
any shortness of breath while ambulating. She was talking freely 
while walking. She stated she was comfortable breathing and 
could go home. 
 
# Pneumonia: Her sputum production and cough symptoms could also 
have been caused by a pneumonia. A repeat chest X-ray displayed 
a minimally increased right lower lobe opacity, likely 
atelectasis, but could represent early or developing infection 
in the clinical setting. She was therefore started on Levaquin 
750mg qd for 5 days. Her WBC were downtrending and normalized

# Hypertension: Patient continued on her home meds of 
lisinopril/ HCTZ
 
# Depression: Pt. continue on her home meds of Sertraline while 
in the hospital

hypothyroidism who presents with 3 episodes of left hand and

speech and hand tingling.  No episodes were associated with 
headache and no history of recent trauma. Of note, a few years 
of minor
memory issues-- forgetting names, misplacing objects, etc.

normal, however she continued to have brief episode similar to 
the ones that brought her in.

Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear ___,

You were admitted to the Neurology Service with episodes of left 
sided paresthesias.  On CT scan you were found to have a small 
bleed that likely caused your symptoms.  On MRI we found that 
you had a small, stable hemorrhage called a subarachnoid 
hemorrhage (SAH).  This kind of blood can sometimes cause brief 
episodes of numbness/tingling like the kind you report.  We do 
not think these were seizures, and we did not give you 
medications to control them. You may continue to have 
intermittent spells like the one that brought you to the 
hospital, but we do not think these are dangerous and are likely 
to improve as your brain heals from this injury.

If you continue to have daily tingling episodes through the 
weekend, please contact Dr. ___ office of Stroke 
Neurology at ___ on ___.

While you were here we sent you for a CT angiogram of the head 
and neck to look at the blood vessels of the brain and there was 
concern that this might be due to a small blood vessel 
abnormality called an AVM (aterio-venous malformation). The 
Neurosurgery team performed an angiogram to evaluate for this 
possibility and found no abnormality.  We think that most likely 
the cause of your hemorrhage is a process called amyloid 
angiopathy.  This is a condition whereby the blood vessels in 
the brain become weak and can rupture, and it is more commonly 
seen in older patients with memory complaints.  Going forward, 
the most important thing you can do is control your blood 
pressure (systolic less than 140).  

Because you had a bleed in your brain, you should not be on 
aspirin or blood thinners of any kind.  Please remain on the 
medications listed in your discharge summary and follow up as 
below

During your admission you were also noted to have intermittent 
fast heart rates, for which you were given a home holter 
monitor, which your primary care doctor ___ follow up.

# Asthma exacerbation
Patient initially presented to an urgent care clinic 4 days 
prior to admission for worsening dyspnea, diagnosed with an 
acute exacerbation of COPD, started on a 5 day course of 
prednisone 40 mg daily and azithromycin. Discharged home with 
brief improvement but subsequent worsening of her dyspnea, 
prompting presentation to ___ ED on ___. Patient was 
found to have significant obstructive lung disease with a peak 
expiratory flow in the emergency department of 150. She was 
given short-acting bronchodilators, IV methylprednisone and 
switched to levofloxacin. Her respiratory status markedly 
improved overnight. Patient had PFTs in ___ showing normal 
FVC and FEV1 and mildly reduced FEV1/FVC, not meeting criteria 
for COPD. Her history and her presentation is more consistent an 
asthma exacerbation, most likely triggered by an acute viral 
infection of the upper respiratory tract, possibly compounded by 
recent discontinuation of Advair. No clear evidence of pneumonia 
on imaging. Repeat except expiratory flow prior to discharge was 
240. In the absence of evidence for pneumonia or another 
infection, levofloxacin was discontinued the patient's treatment 
course with azithromycin was completed. Prednisone 40 mg daily 
should be continued for another 5 days in total after 
presentation to ___ (last day ___. Advair 100/50 was 
restarted. Albuterol was continued. Follow-up with pulmonology 
for further titration of the bronchodilators is recommended.

History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is a ___ man w/ history of DVT/PE s/p 
prostatectomy who presents with back and L calf pain x 1 week.  
He has had chronic low back pain for approximately ___ years, 
with occasional radiation down his legs, bilateral numbness and 
tingling.  Pt says he has been evaluated by his PCP and may have 
been diagnosed with sciatica. The patient reports that about 6 
months ago this back pain began taking on a different 
characteristic, from sharp radiating pain to a dull constant 
backache which worsens from sitting to standing.  His back pain 
is not worse at night. He is followed regularly by his urologist 
at ___, and last year had a PSA which was 1.0 
by his report.  Approximately 2 weeks ago, his back ache was 
exacerbated when he strained his muscles while moving out of his 
house.  One week prior to admission, he noticed L thigh pain; 
his PCP prescribed ___ with progressive worsening of 
symptoms.  The patient endorsed bedrest over the past week, and 
yesterday was unable to walk secondary to pain.  Also developed 
left calf swelling during this time.  He was then referred to 
the ED by his PCP.  
.
Of note, the patient had a prior provoked DVT and PE in ___ two 
days s/p prostatectomy for prostate cancer.  At the time, he 
experienced an episode of syncope while walking and was 
subsequently found to have a pulmonary embolus and DVT.  He was 
placed on anticoagulation with warfarin for 9 months and patient 
reports IVC filter placement as well.  In addition, the patient 
endorses a general feeling of "not feeling right" for the past 
six months.  Per his brother, he is now living with family due 
to financial troubles and during this time has had a noticeable 
decline in cognitive function.  Examples of this decline include 
inappropriate or circumlocutory responses to questions, and 
episodes of confusion.  He has also had a few episodes of 
dizziness on standing, resolving spontaneously without LOC.   
.
He denies any leg trauma, chest pain, SOB, palpitations, weight 
loss, urinary or stool incontinence, venous ulcers, family 
history of clots, recent smoking history, recent changes in 
medication, hematochezia, hematuria, hemoptysis.
. 
In the ED VS T 100.1, HR 102, BP 136/54, RR 14, O2 97% RA. 
Ultrasound showed a DVT in the left lower extremity. He was 
started on a heparin drip and admitted to the floor.  On arrival 
to the floor he reported still having some calf pain but denied 
other complaints.  
.

dry cough secondary to post-nasal drip. 
Negative for CP, SOB, palpitations, abdominal pain, nausea, 
vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, or other arthralgias or myalgias.  
No HA or blurry vision at present.   Denies any recent bloody or 
dark tarry stools.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ is a ___ man w/ history of provoked DVT/PE 
s/p prostatectomy who presented with back and L calf pain x 1 
week, and who was found to have an occlusive DVT in the LLE, 
with non-occlusive DVT in RLE.

ACTIVE ISSUES:

#. DVT - The patient was found on ultrasound to have diffuse 
occlusive clot in the left lower extremity, as well as 
non-occlusive clot in the right common femoral and proximal 
right superficial femoral vein.  He was started on IV heparin 
gtt in the ED, and later transitioned to enoxaparin 100mg SC 
Q12H on the floor.  Warfarin 5 mg po daily was started ___, 
and patient was continued on enoxaparin as bridge.  Although he 
was tachycardic on presentation, his tachycardia resolved on the 
floor and he had no complaints of dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, 
or recurrent dizziness.  He remained hemodynamically stable, and 
had no RV heave or JVD on exam.  EKG demonstrated NSR and was 
unremarkable.   A targeted malignancy work-up was performed 
given his history of prostate cancer; PSA was <0.1 and lumbar 
spine X-ray showed no evidence of pathologic fractures.  It was 
thought that prolonged bedrest in the setting of 
acute-on-chronic back pain, as well as his IVC ___ have 
contributed to the development of spontaneous DVT.  On HD#2 the 
patient endorsed moderate improvement of LLE swelling, although 
he continued to have moderate pain with weight-bearing. He was 
seen by physical therapy, who cleared him for discharge home. 
The patient was discharged on warfarin, and will continue on 
enoxaparin BID until his INR remains therapeutic on warfarin.  
He will have INR checked ___, with results faxed to his PCP. 
 The patient's brother (primary caregiver) was instructed on 
lovenox administration.  Patient was also discharged with OT 
services for home safety evaluation. He will continue to take 
tylenol as needed for pain, and was advised to avoid NSAID use 
while on anticoagulation.  Patient should remain up-to-date on 
age-appropriate cancer screening.

#. Orthostatic hypotension - On day of admission, patient found 
to be significantly orthostatic (lying 129/80, HR 88 --> 
standing 77/64, HR 134).  His orthostatic hypotension was felt 
to be secondary to hypovolemia in setting of recent decreased PO 
intake, and resolved after administration of IVF.  Patient's 
dizziness upon standing prior to admission was likely secondary 
to orthostatic hypotension.

#. Mental status change - Per family and PCP, patient has had 
progressive confusion over the past several months. During this 
hospitalization the patient exhibited confusion with 
word-finding and short-term memory difficulties. Chem 10, 
vitamin B12, folate, TSH, RPR labs were obtained to assess for 
reversible causes of dementia; RPR still pending at time of 
discharge but other labs were WNL.  Patient did have 
leukocytosis on admission, but this was felt to be secondary to 
inflammation in setting of DVT, and not due to an infectious 
process that might also be contributing to AMS.  UA and CXR 
unremarkable, and WBC trended down to WNL prior to discharge.  
The patient had no focal neurologic findings suggestive of a 
space-occupying lesion. It was thought that the patient's recent 
self-holding of antidepressive medications and underlying 
depression/anxiety could contribute to a pseudodementia picture, 
or that the patient may be developing early dementia.  The 
patient's PCP was notified of this clinical picture and the 
patient will follow-up on an outpatient basis.  He may benefit 
from MRI imaging of the brain, and may also benefit from a 
cognitive neurology consultation. 

#. Low back pain - The patient's back pain is likely 
acute-on-chronic, in the context of mechanical strain s/p recent 
heavy lifting.  Given history of prostate cancer, we evaluated 
for possible metastatic spinal lesions; PSA< 0.1 and the absence 
of midline spinal tenderness were reassuring.  Neuro exam was 
WNL, and rectal exam demonstrated normal tone.  Lumbar plain 
film showed anterolisthesis only, which has been noted on prior 
imaging.  The patient did not complain of back pain during this 
hospitalization. 

#. Leukocytosis - The patient had leukocytosis (WBC 14.0) and 
low grade temperature on admission. This was likely most likely 
secondary to venous thromboembolism, as the patient was without 
signs or symptoms of infection.  UA/urine culture unremarkable, 
and CXR did not demonstrate evidence of PNA.  His leukocytosis 
resolved prior to discharge.

#. ___: Cr 1.2 on presentation, and trended down to 1.0 
following IVF administration.  Patient may have had mild 
pre-renal azotemia in setting of dehydration.

#. Diabetes ___: Patient without prior history of DM, 
though HbA1c 6.7 this admission.  Patient will follow-up with 
his PCP for further evaluation and work-up of newly diagnosed 
diabetes ___.  

INACTIVE ISSUES:

#. Anxiety/depression - Patient previously on escitalopram and 
oxazepam as an outpatient, but has been off these medications 
for at least one month.  He self-discontinued these meds, likely 
as he was no longer able to afford them.  He received oxazepam 
in the hospital as needed for anxiety.  

#. h/o Prostate cancer - Given concern for prostate cancer 
metastasis in the setting of low back pain and mental status 
changes, we checked a PSA level which was reassuring at <0.1.  
He will continue to be followed closely by his regular urologist 
at ___. 

#. h/o PUD:  Patient continued on home regimen omeprazole 20mg 
daily.
.
#. Hyperlipidemia: Continued on simvastatin at home dose of 20mg 
daily.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ year old female with advanced dementia presenting to ___ 
from her facility after a fall. Imaging revealed a right sided 
rib fracture. Urinalysis was also positive so she was started on 
antibiotics. She was admitted to the Trauma service for pain 
management and ___ evaluation.

Tertiary survey was negative for additional injuries. Physical 
Therapy recommended rehab once medically cleared. Pain was well 
controlled. The patient voided without problem. During this 
hospitalization, the patient ambulated early and frequently with 
assist, required assist with respiratory toilet and incentive 
spirometry.. The patient received subcutaneous heparin and 
venodyne boots were used during this stay. She completed a 
course of antibiotics for the UTI. 

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, out of bed with assist, incontinent of urine, and pain was 
well controlled. Respirations were unlabored and oxygen 
saturation was 94% on room air. The patient was discharged to 
rehab. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up 
instructions but due to her dementia she was unable to fully 
verbalize understanding.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to ___ after a fall. You fractured a rib on 
the right side. You have worked with ___ and they recommend 
rehab. You are medically stable and ready for discharge. 

* Your injury caused a rib fractures which can cause severe pain 
and subsequently cause you to take shallow breaths because of 
the pain.
 
* You should take your pain medication as directed to stay ahead 
of the pain otherwise you won't be able to take deep breaths. If 
the pain medication is too sedating take half the dose and 
notify your physician.
 
* Pneumonia is a complication of rib fractures.  In order to 
decrease your risk you must use your incentive spirometer 4 
times every hour while awake.  This will help expand the small 
airways in your lungs and assist in coughing up secretions that 
pool in the lungs.
 
* You will be more comfortable if you use a cough pillow to hold 
against your chest and guard your rib cage while coughing and 
deep breathing.
 
* Symptomatic relief with ice packs or heating pads for short 
periods may ease the pain.
 
* Narcotic pain medication can cause constipation therefore you 
should take a stool softener twice daily and increase your fluid 
and fiber intake if possible.
 
* Do NOT smoke
 
* If your doctor allows, non-steroidal ___ drugs 
are very effective in controlling pain ( ie, Ibuprofen, Motrin, 
Advil, Aleve, Naprosyn) but they have their own set of side 
effects so make sure your doctor approves.
 
* Return to the Emergency Room right away for any acute 
shortness of breath, increased pain or crackling sensation 
around your ribs (crepitus).

 
Followup Instructions:
___

CT ABD/PELVIS IMPRESSION: 
1. Marked intrahepatic biliary dilatation. Diffuse infiltrative 
mass in the majority of the right lobe of the liver, 
incompletely assessed on this single phase study. This may be 
consistent with cholangiocarcinoma or metastatic disease.  
2. Diverticulosis without diverticulitis. 
3. Within the subcutaneous fat extending to the overlying skin 
is a 2.4 x 1.1 cm soft tissue density nodule with likely 
enhancement. Please correlate with direct visualization.  

RUQ U/S IMPRESSION:

malignancy. Recommend multiphasic CT to further assess.

3. Cholelithiasis. 

Cytology COMMON BILE DUCT BRUSHINGS   ___ ___ 
Logged Only

Contrast medium was injected resulting in 
opacification(cannulation) 
The common bile duct and common hepatic duct were not dilated. 
No filling defects were seen. 
The right hepatic system was not filled with contrast. 
A wire was passed through a stricture in the left intrahepatic 
duct into the left hepatic system and the ducts were filled with 
contrast. 
There was post-obstructive dilation.   
A sphincterotomy was performed in the 12 o'clock position using 
a sphincterotome over an existing guidewire.  
Cytology samples were obtained for histology from the stricture 
using a brush. 
A 5cm by ___ double pig tail biliary stent was placed 
successfully(sphincterotomy, cytology, stent placement) 
Otherwise normal ercp to third part of the duodenum
 
Recommendations: No aspirin, plavix, NSAIDS, coumadin for 72 
hours.

instigated, placement of a metal stent can be arranged.

Name:  ___                   Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: MEDICINE
 
Allergies: 
Penicillins
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
Pericardial effusion with early tamponade physiology 
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
pericardial tap and drain placement

 
History of Present Illness:
___ year-old male with RA recently started on MTX, HTN, IDDM, 
COPD, admitted to CCU after found to have new afib with RVR and 
pericardial effusion.  
.  
Patient went to PCP on day of admission with fatigue, vague 
symptoms (belching, substernal discomfort relieved by rubbing 
epigastrum and Tums) for several weeks, without chest pain, 
pleuritic pain, dyspnea, palpitations, PND, orthopnea. He 
reported lower extremity edema which has been present for ___ 
years. Fatigue was characterized by decreased exercise 
tolerance; whereas he could previously climb 20 stairs without 
problem, he now had to stop after 10 stairs. He attributed all 
symptoms to methotrexate which he started two weeks prior. He 
also denied fever, chills, cough, diarrhea, dysuria, or sick 
contacts. ECG in office in new afib with rate of 120-130s and 
low voltage with stable BP. Sent to ___, CTA negative 
for PE or dissection but significant for pericardial effusion 
with ?RV collapse on bedside ECHO. Given IVF and started briefly 
dopamine for SBP ___, transferred to ___ ___ for further 
evaluation and care.  
.  
In ___ ___, initial vitals were 98.0, HR 98, 167/100, 19, 100% 
on 2L. Patient was off dopamine prior to admission at our ___ 
with SBP 130-140s. Pulsus of ___, exam significant for distant 
heart sounds but no rub. ECG with low voltage. Bedside ECHO by 
cardiology fellow showed moderate concentric effusion, with some 
RA invagination but no RV collapse or septal bowing. Patient 
admitted to CCU for monitoring overnight, plan for pericardial 
tap in AM.  
.  
On transfer from ___, vitals were HR 99, 111/86, 24, 97% on 2L. 
Patient reports feeling well. Denies SOB, chest discomfort at 
this time. Asking to eat. 

 
Past Medical History:
1. CARDIAC RISK FACTORS: Diabetes(+), Dyslipidemia(?), 
Hypertension(+)  
2. CARDIAC HISTORY:  
-CABG: None  
-PERCUTANEOUS CORONARY INTERVENTIONS: None  
-PACING/ICD: None  
3. OTHER PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:  
- Rheumatoid arthritis: Diagnosed within the past one year. 
Usually takes Tylenol for pain control. Recently (within the 
past one month) was started on methotrexate.  
- COPD  

 
Social History:
___
Family History:
No FH of RA.  
Denies history of early MI.  
 
Physical Exam:
98.5, 98, 141/74, 97% RA, pulsus 16 (138-122)  
General - Resting comfortably in bed, no acute distress  
HEENT - Sclera anicteric, MMM  
Neck - Supple, JVP to ear lobe when HOB 45 degress; unable to 
assess if JVP rises with inspiration  
Pulm - Diffuse expiratory wheezes; no crackles  
CV - Decreased breath sounds; RRR, normal S1/S2; no murmurs, 
rubs  
Abdomen - Obese; normoactive bowel sounds; soft, non-tender  
Ext - Warm, well perfused, radial and DP pulses 2+; 1+ lower 
extremity edema to knees bilaterally  
Neuro - CNII-XII intact; upper and lower extremity strength ___ 
and equal bilaterally  

 
Pertinent Results:
EKG (___):  Sinus rhythm. Low voltage. ST-T wave 
abnormalities. No previous tracing available for comparison. 
Clinical correlation is suggested.

TTE (___):  Left ventricular wall thicknesses and cavity 
size are normal. Due to suboptimal technical quality, a focal 
wall motion abnormality cannot be fully excluded. Overall left 
ventricular systolic function is normal (LVEF 50-60%). Right 
ventricular chamber size and free wall motion are normal. No 
aortic regurgitation is seen. No mitral regurgitation is seen. 
There is a small to moderate sized pericardial effusion. The 
effusion is echo dense, consistent with blood, inflammation or 
other cellular elements. No right ventricular diastolic collapse 
is seen. There is brief right atrial diastolic invagination 
consistent elevated intrapericardial pressure without overt 
tamponade. IMPRESSION: Small to moderate circumfirential 
pericardial effusion. No overt tamponade.

Cardiac catheterization (___): 

EKG (___):  Probable atrial flutter with variable but 
predeominantly 2:1 block versus atrial fibrillation. Since the 
previous tracing the atrial tachy-arrhythmia is new. Clinical 
correlation is suggested.

TTE (___):  Overall left ventricular systolic function is 
normal (LVEF>55%). RV with normal free wall contractility. There 
is a small pericardial effusion. There are no echocardiographic 
signs of tamponade. Compared with the prior study (images 
reviewed) of ___, the pericardial effusion is now smaller.

CXR 1V (___):  Catheter placement as described. Fiding 
consistent with subsegmental atelectasis on the left. A 
retrocardiac infiltrate cannot be excluded.

On admission (___):
WBC-12.2* Hgb-10.0* Hct-30.8* MCV-77* Plt ___

TotProt-5.1 Glucose-4 LD(LDH)-4230 Amylase-19 Albumin-2.___ year-old male with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, 
recently started on methotrexate, hypertension, 
insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, and COPD with new diagnosis 
atrial fibrillation and pericardial effusion. Hospital course 
was as follows.

1. Pericardial effusion:  On presentation, patient displayed 
signs of early cardiac tamponade with RA bowing without RV 
dysfunction on TTE. He was tachycardic, normotensive, and with a 
pulsus of 16. JVD was difficult to assess given body habitus. 
EKG was with low voltage and without electrical alernans. 
Although elevated pulsus and low voltage on EKG can be 
concerning for tamponade, both can also be seen in COPD. Patient 
was taken for pericardiocentesis with drain placement on 
___. Right heart catheterization revealed equalization of 
pressures: RA 26, RVEDP 26, and PCPWP 28. Approximately 300cc of 
serous fluid was removed. Drain was removed after 24 hours. 
Given exudate with low glucose, effusion was thought to be 
secondary to rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatology was consulted, 
and recommended holding methotrexate and increasing prednisone 
to 30mg PO daily. Pericardial cultures remained no growth to 
date on discharge. Cytology is pending at time of discharge.
Pt needs to have a repeat ECHO in approx 1 month.  

2. Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation:  Newly diagnosed atrial 
fibrillation at PCP's office on day of admission. Was in RVR 
with rate in 120-130s. CHADS2 score is 3 (hypertension, age, 
DMII). Patient was in and out of atrial fibrillation during 
hospital course. He initially was responsive to metoprolol for 
rate control. During one episode he required metoprolol, a 
diltiazem gtt, and a amiodarone gtt. Diltiazem, and amiodarone 
were stopped following removal of drain and patient was 
restarted on verapamil per home regimen for rate control with 
low dose metoprolol. He was also started on coumadin 5mg PO 
daily for anticoagulation. Will be followed in ___ 
clinic at ___. 

3. Hypertension:  Blood pressure well-controlled throughout 
hospital course. Home medications include verapamil, Benicar, 
and Maxide. Patient was restarted on verapamil. Benicar and 
Maxide were restarted on discharge. 

4. Dyslipidemia:  Continued statin per home regimen.

5. Rheumatoid arthrtitis:  Seropositive. Recently diagnosed. On 
methotrexate, prednisone, and acetaminophen at home. Per 
rheumatology recs, sent ___, SSA, SSB, RNP, Sm, anti-CCP, SPEP 
to confirmed diagnosis and rule out other potential diagnoses 
such as Sjogren's and SLE. As above, methotrexate was 
discontinued and prednisone increased to 30mg PO daily.

6. Acute kidney injury:  Baseline creatinine unknown. Creatinine 
1.6 on admission, improved to 1.0 on discharge. Improvement 
likely due to better forward flow following pericardiocentesis.

7. COPD:  Underlying etiology unknown; patient denies history of 
tobacco use. Continued Advair, ipratropium per home regimen. 

8. Diabetes mellitus:  Insulin-dependent. Home regimen included 
28NPH/12Regular QAM, 13Regular with dinner, 30NPH QHS. Blood 
glucose remained high in context of steroid therapy. Insulin 
regimen was altered to 36u NPH BID for duration of high-dose 
prednisone therapy but when called on day after discharge, BS 
were low in am and Actos was restarted. Decreased NPH back to 
previous home regimen. 

9. Glaucoma:  Continued prednisonolone drops, Thera tears per 
home regimen.

10. GERD:  Continued Prilosec 20mg PO daily per home regimen.

11. Micorcytic Anemia:  Microcytic. Hematocrit stable around ___enies gross blood in stools. On folate 
at home. Iron studies revealed mild Fe deficiency. 

 
Medications on Admission:
Insulin 28NPH/12Regular, 13Regular with dinner, 30NPH QHS
Reglan 10mg PO daily
Prilosec 20mg PO daily
Maxide 75mg PO daily
Verapamil 240mg PO BID
Benicar 10mg PO daily
Pravastatin 20mg PO QHS
ASA 81mg PO daily
Prednisone 10mg po BID
Methotrexate 2.5mg 4xweekly
Folate 1mg PO daily
Magnesium PO daily
Prednisolone drops
Thera tears
Advair as needed
Atrovent as needed

 
Discharge Medications:
1. Outpatient Lab Work
Please check your INR on ___ at the ___

Discharge Instructions:
You had a pericardial effusion, or fluid collection around your 
heart. We think this is because of your rheumatoid arthritis and 
possibly because of your methotrexate. The cell analysis of the 
fluid is still pending. A pericardial drain was placed and 
removed after all the fluid was gone. You will need another 
echocardiogram in approximately 1 month to make sure the fluid 
hasn't returned. You can hae this arranged to be done at 
___ by Dr. ___. We increased your prednisone and 
stopped the methotrexate. You will need to see Dr. ___ on 
___ to discuss the medication changes. 
You also are in atrial fibrillation, a very common irregular 
heart rhythm. We have started Metoprolol to control the heart 
rate and coumadin to prevent a stroke. 
Medication changes:
1. STOP taking methotrexate
2. Increase the prednisone to 30 mg daily
3. START taking coumadin to prevent a blood clot and stroke from 
the atrial fibrillation. 
4. START taking Metoprolol to control your heart rate. 
.

that brought you to the hospital. Please also call with any 
chest pain, trouble breathing, increased wheezing, fevers or any 
other concerning symptoms.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ with PMH of DM, AFib on coumadin, and RA complicated by 
pericardial effusion and tamponade in ___, presents with 
recurrent pericardial effusion concerning of tamponade and 
complaints of some progressive shortness of breath.

#. Pericardial effusion - patient has had history of pericardial 
effusion in the past, thought to be a complicaton of RA. Had a 
pericardiocentesis with drain placement in ___. Initially had 
methotrexate discontinued for concerns of worsening pericardial 
effusion, but was restarted back on it in ___ when patient did 
not tolerate Humira well. Patient has been receiving regular 
follow up with his cardiologist with a repeat echo every 6 
weeks.  His echo read done at ___ showed a small to moderate 
mainly anterior pericardial effusion with evidence of diastolic 
right ventricular collapse suggestive of tamponade.  An 
additional read done by cardiology service here showed small 
amount of fluid with no evidence of RV diastolic collapse.  He 
was hemodynamically stable during his admission and breathing 
was not labored, although reported to be slightly worse over the 
last few weeks.  Pulsus was checked twice a day and remained 
consistently around ___.  Methotrexate was held given the 
reaccumulation of pericardial fluid.  Patient was evaluated by 
thoracic surgery for pericardial window.  Procedure was 
performed on ___ with drainage of only 80cc of fluid and 
was complicated by afib with RVR. 
The drainage tube was subsequently removed and he is being 
followed with serial imaging which at the time of discharge 
showed a smaller pericardial effusion on echo and only 
atelectasis on CXR. Fluid culture was positive for 
propionobacterium acnes which was felt to be a contaminant. 
Biopsy from pericardial window showed unremarkable cartilage and 
skeletal muscle and fragments of pericardium with chronic 
inflammation and fibrin deposition.
.
#. A fib - patient takes warfarin at home, was found to be 
supratherapeutic at INR of 3.7 on admission. INR was reversed 
for the pericardial window. He was bridged on heparin drip to 
coumadin and the coumadin alone was continued once his INR was 
therapeutic. 
.
#. HTN - patient was continued on home dosage of metoprolol, 
furosemide, and spironolactone during admission.  Valsartan was 
substituted for his home dose of olmesartan during admission, 
but patient was switched back to olmesartan on discharge.
.
# Anemia: he was found to have a microcytic anemia consistent 
with anemia of chronic disease. An outpatient colonoscopy can be 
considered as an outpatient.
.
#. Hyperlipidemia - patient was continued on home regimen of 
pravastatin
.
#. Rheumatoid Arthritis - patient's home regimen of methotrexate 
was held for pericardial effusion.  Was restarted on discharge 
after his pericardial window procedure.
.
#. COPD - currently at baseline, patient was in no respiratory 
distress.  During admission he was maintained on home dose of 
spiriva.  Advair was substituted for symbicort while admitted, 
but patient was restarted on symbicort on discharge.
.
#. DM - patient was continued on his home regimen of insulin as 
well as regular insulin sliding scale.
.
#. GERD - patient was continued on omeprazole
.
#. Glaucoma - patient was continued on prednisolone drop to left 
eye and artificial tears

Name:  ___                  Unit No:   ___

History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is a ___ man who is maintained on 
hemodialysis via a left
forearm loop graft.  The graft thrombosed several months ago and 
was successfully thrombectomized.  He presented to AV Care last 
week with concerns for growing aneurysms on the arterial limb of 
the access.  He reports no difficulties with dialysis, but does 
note some pain associated with the graft.  He has had no fevers 
or chills.  He did receive one dose of IV vancomycin last week. 
He denies any neuro symptoms related to his hand.
 
Past Medical History:
end-stage renal disease, HIV, hepatitis C, and mitral and 
tricuspid valvular disorder. The patient reports that he has a 
___ valve and that he is on Coumadin for this.  The records 
available do not confirm that he has had valve surgery.
 
Social History:
___
Family History:
NC
 
Physical Exam:
Temperature is 98.0, heart rate 56, blood pressure 115/62, and 
his weight is 58.6 kg.  He has a faintly palpable radial pulse 
and an overlying incision from an attempted radiocephalic 
fistula.  The forearm loop graft itself is patent
and is slightly pulsatile.  There is a good thrill in the 
outflow vein in the mid upper arm.  There are several areas of 
aneurysmal dilatation on the arterial limb.  The skin is mobile 
without ulceration.  There is minimal erythema around the more 
distal aneurysm.  His lungs are clear to auscultation 
bilaterally.  His heart is regular without any type of 
mechanical heart sounds.  He has no edema on exam today.

and is along the medial aspect of left forearm. Topical

Pt was continued on his home pain regimen and was given PO 
dilaudid for breakthrough pain. Pt tolerated regular diet 
throughout admission, was able to ambulate without difficulty 
and was in stable medical condition at time of discharge.

Pt was not informed of any future appointment dates or follow-up

Discharge Instructions:
Mr. ___, 

We appreciated the opportunity to participate in your care at 
___. You were admitted to our hospital due to a concern that 
your AV fistula graft was infected. Our transplant surgery team, 
who places these types of grafts, evaluated the possible 
infected area of your graft, but did not think this was the 
cause to your symptoms. They did not remove the graft from your 
arm as initially planned. 

We completed an evaluation for other possible sources of 
infection that could be causing your symptoms.  This has 
included imaging of your chest, blood and urine cultures, and an 
echocardiogram of your heart which shows no signs of infection.  
At this point, it is unclear what the cause of your infection 
was, but regardless it has resolved.

An EKG showed that your heart experiences an abnormality called 
long QT which may be caused by certain medications.  Prozac is 
one of these medications, so it is advised that you stop taking 
it.

You will need to follow up with your primary care physician 
within the next week after you have been discharged. 

Please make the following changes to your medications: 

STOP taking this medication: 
- prozac

Take all of your other medications as before.  Your INR will be 
monitored closely and your coumadin dose may be adjusted as per 
this result.

had a prolonged intensive care unit course.  His intensive care 
unit course is summarized below.

___: The patient was admitted to the surgical intensive care 
unit following his first exploratory laparotomy. A ___ 
catheter was placed. Echocardiography showed only inferior wall 
motion, and severe tricuspid regurgitation. A Swan-Ganz catheter 
was unable to be placed due to the severe tricuspid 
regurgitation. He was maintained on pressors and transfused 1u 
PRBC. Nitric oxide was attempted to decrease pulmnoary arterial 
hypertension without improvement and was hence discontinued. His 
ventilatory status improved somewhat, with a max PEEP of 15 
decreasing to 10. His lactate continued to rise, and he had 
persistent tachycardia with an unclear source. 
___: The patient's cordis was re-wired to a triple lumen.  Hid

requirements of levophed and milrinone.
