Name:  ___                 Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: UROLOGY
 
Allergies: 
Penicillins
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
Prostate Cancer
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Robotic Assisted Laparoscopic Prostatectomy

 
History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is a ___ M who was followed by Dr.
___ his prostate cancer.  In ___, his PSA on 
finasteride was 5.29, and a biopsy showed ___ 6 in seven 
cores.  This biopsy was rereviewed by our ___ pathology team, who 
thought one of the sections at the right mid was more consistent 
with ___ 3+4 disease.  Nevertheless,
there is large volume tumor on the right side with all sections 
involved and tumor on the left only in the left mid section less 
than 5% of each core.  He was moving towards a prostatectomy, 
but he has coronary artery disease, a coronary event in ___ forced him to postpone this, and because of stent placement 
was placed on Plavix and aspirin.  He is here for surgical 
managment of his prostate cancer. 

 
Past Medical History:
Ulcerative colitis
Cardiac stent in ___
Urinary retention in ___
Dog bite to left calf in ___ leading to DVT.  Coumadin toxicity 
with INR of 10. 
IVC filter placed ___.  
___, NSTEMI.  
___, prostate biopsy as above. 
Dupuytren's contracture.   
Angioplasty in ___.  
Erectile dysfunction.   
Thrombocytopenia, possibly secondary to ETOH.  
Orthostatic hypotension
Osteoarthritis
45-pack-year smoking history, quit ___ years ago
Umbilical hernia.

 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Positive for prostate cancer in his father, who had a 
prostatectomy ___ years ago and is alive and well.

 
Physical Exam:
WdWn male, NAD, AVSS
Interactive, cooperative
Abdomen soft, appropriately tender along incisions
Incisions c/d/i w/out evidence hematoma, infection
Extremities w/out edema or pitting and no report of calf pain
Foley catheter in place, secured to medial thigh; urine yellow 
and clear

 
Pertinent Results:
___ 06:40AM BLOOD Glucose-125* UreaN-9 Creat-0.8 Na-138 
K-4.0 Cl-103 HCO3-30 AnGap-9
___ 06:40AM BLOOD WBC-5.0 RBC-3.38*# Hgb-11.8*# Hct-34.2*# 
MCV-101* MCH-35.0* MCHC-34.6 RDW-13.2 Plt ___
 
Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ was admitted to the Urology Service after undergoing 
robotic assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy. No concerning 
intra-operative events occurred; please see dictated operative 
note for full details.  The patient received ___ 
antibiotic prophylaxis.  At the end of the procedure the patient 
was extubated and transported to the PACU for further recovery 
before being transferred to the floor.  He was transferred from 
the PACU in stable condition. On POD0, pain was well controlled 
on PCA, hydrated with intravenous fluids for urine output 
>30cc/hour, provided with pneumoboots and incentive spirometry 
for prophylaxis, and he ambulated once. On POD1, the patient was 
restarted on home medications, basic metabolic panel and 
complete blood count were checked, pain control was transitioned 
from PCA to oral analgesics, diet was advanced to a clears/toast 
and crackers diet for breakfast and lunch.  Diet was advanced to 
regular after lunch and with further ambulation, the JP drain 
was removed.  Foley catheter care and leg bag teaching was 
provided by nursing. The remainder of the hospital course was 
unremarkable. The patient was discharged in stable condition, 
eating well, ambulating independently, and with pain control on 
oral analgesics. On exam, incision was clean, dry, and intact, 
with no evidence of hematoma collection or infection. The 
patient was given explicit instructions to follow-up in one week 
for post-operative evaluation and trial of voiding.

 
Medications on Admission:
Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY    
Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY (off 5 days prior to surgery) 
Metoprolol Succinate XL 12.5 mg PO DAILY  
Rosuvastatin Calcium 10 mg PO DAILY  

 
Discharge Medications:
1. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 
2. Bacitracin Ointment 1 Appl TP TID 
apply to penile meatus 
RX *bacitracin zinc 500 unit/gram apply to urethral meatus BIDw 
Disp #*1 Tube Refills:*0
3. Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY 
start ___ in am 
4. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 
Start: POD #1 
RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth twice a day 
Disp #*40 Capsule Refills:*0
5. Metoprolol Succinate XL 12.5 mg PO DAILY 
Start tonight ___ if not already given.  please hold for sbp 
<100, hr <60 
6. Oxycodone-Acetaminophen (5mg-325mg) 1 TAB PO Q4H:PRN 
breakthrough pain only(score>4) 
only to begin after PCA is discontinued 
RX *oxycodone-acetaminophen 5 mg-325 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth 
every six (6) hours Disp #*20 Tablet Refills:*0
7. Rosuvastatin Calcium 10 mg PO DAILY 
8. Ciprofloxacin HCl 500 mg PO Q12H 
Begin first dose the day prior to foley catheter removal 
RX *ciprofloxacin 250 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth every twelve (12) 
hours Disp #*6 Tablet Refills:*0
9. Acetaminophen 500 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain 
do not exceed more than 4 grams daily (including acetaminophen 
in narcotics) 
RX *acetaminophen [Acetaminophen Extra Strength] 500 mg 1 
tablet(s) by mouth every six (6) hours Disp #*40 Tablet 
Refills:*0

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Prostate Cancer

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
-Please also reference the additional handout provided by Dr. 
___  with instructions and information about your surgery and 
post-operative plan of care.

-Please also review the provided handout w/nursing instructions 
on Foley catheter care and leg bag usage

-To reduce the strain/pressure on your abdomen and incision 
sites; remember to log roll onto your side and then use your 
hands to push yourself upright while taking advantage of the 
momentum of putting your legs/feet to the ground. 

-Resume your pre-admission/home medications except as noted. 
Always call to inform, review and discuss any medication changes 
and your post-operative course with your primary care doctor

-___ stated, you may resume your pre-admission medications except 
as noted but you no longer need to take medications that shrink 
your prostate (Hytrin, Avodart, etc.) unless advised to continue 
them by the urologist

-Please AVOID supplements that may have blood-thinning effects 
(like Fish Oil, Vitamin E, etc.) until your Foley has been 
removed or you are otherwise explicitly advised 

-FIRST, take Tylenol (acetaminophen) for pain control.  

-REPLACE the Tylenol with the prescribed narcotic if the 
narcotic is combined with Tylenol (examples include brand names 
___, Tylenol #3 w/ codeine and their generic 
equivalents).  ALWAYS discuss your medications (especially when 
using narcotics or new medications) use with the pharmacist when 
you first retrieve your prescription if you have any questions.  
  Use the narcotic pain medication for break-through pain that 
is >4 on the pain scale.

-The MAXIMUM dose of Tylenol (ACETAMINOPHEN) is 4 grams (from 
ALL sources) PER DAY and remember that the prescribed narcotic 
pain medication may also contain Tylenol (acetaminophen) so this 
needs to be considered when monitoring your daily dose and 
maximum.

-Please do NOT drive, operate dangerous machinery, or consume 
alcohol while taking narcotic pain medications.

-For your safety and the safety of others; PLEASE DO NOT drive, 
operate dangerous machinery, or consume alcohol while taking 
narcotic pain medications. Do NOT drive while Foley catheter/Leg 
bag are in place.

-Colace has been prescribed to avoid post surgical constipation 
and constipation related to narcotic pain medication. 
Discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. Colace is a 
stool softener--it is NOT a laxative.

-You may shower 2 days after surgery, but do not tub bathe, 
swim, soak, or scrub incision for 2 weeks

-If you had a drain removed from your abdomen, bandage strips 
called steristrips have been applied to close the wound. Allow 
these bandage strips to fall off on their own over time but 
please REMOVE the gauze dressing in 2 days. You may get the 
steristrips wet.

-No heavy lifting for 4 weeks (no more than 10 pounds)

-Your Foley should be secured to the catheter secure on your 
thigh at ALL times until your follow up with the surgeon.

-Follow up in 1 week for wound check and Foley removal. DO NOT 
have anyone else other than your Surgeon remove your Foley for 
any reason.

-Wear Large Foley bag for majority of time, leg bag is only for 
short-term when leaving house.

-Start prescribed antibiotic (Ciprofloxacin) 1 day prior to 
scheduled Foley catheter removal and for two subsequent days

-Resume your regular home diet and remember to drink plenty of 
fluids to keep hydrated and to prevent constipation

-___ medical attention for fevers (temp>101.5), worsening pain, 
drainage or excessive bleeding from incision, chest pain or 
shortness of breath.
 
Followup Instructions:
___

Discharge Diagnosis:
Left shoulder DJD

 
Discharge Condition:
Stable

 
Discharge Instructions:
Wound Care:
Keep steri-strips in place until they fall off or they are 
removed at your first post-operative visit.
Showering in 3 days is fine; be sure to pat the incision dry 
afterwards.
Do not scrub the area; just allow water/soap to wash over you.
Do not bath or swim approved by surgeon.

Ice:
We recommend that you use the ice wrap/cryocuff on a consistent 
basis for the first 2448 hours. This will help reduce 
post-operative swelling. After that, use as necessary. 

Sling & Weight bearing status:
With Total Shoulder Replacement, you will be placed in a sling.
You need to wear the sling for ___ weeks.  It should only be 
removed for showering and for your exercises.
You should not bear weight with your arm or use your arm to lift 
anything until 3 months after surgery.

Physical Therapy: 
Formal physical therapy will be ordered by your orthopedic 
surgeon in the form of a written prescription upon discharge 
from surgery. You will also receive a therapy protocol form.  
Following Total shoulder replacement, you do not need formal 
physical therapy initially.  Your surgeon will instruct you when 
you can move your shoulder. 

you can do some exercises at home, such as:  pendulum exercises 
(see pictures below).  You should also bend + straighten your 
elbow and move your wrist so they will not get stiff.  You 
should not lift your arm using your shoulder muscles until 
directed by your surgeon.

Pain Control:
You may find it beneficial to sleep in a recliner or propped up 
on pillows for the first several days after surgery.

Narcotics
You have been prescribed narcotic pain medications. Use the 
medication only as prescribed. Take it with food. Do not drive 
or use any type of heavy machinery, drink alcohol, make any 
life-changing or legal decisions (i.e. sign a will), or 
participate in activities that require a lot of physical 
skill.Take a stool softener, such as Colace, while taking the 
narcotic medication, because narcotics may cause constipation.

Non- Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Medications:
Please take an over the counter non-steroidal anti-inflammatory 
such as Motrin, Advil, Ibuprofen or Aleve (600 mg every 12 
hours) for additional pain control for two weeks and then as 
needed.Take only if you do not have a contraindication to taking 
this medication (i.e. allergy to the medication, stomach ulcers 
or kidney problems).
Take this medication with food, and try to stagger the times you 
take this medication with your pain medication.  This may help 
decrease the chance of stomach upset.

Driving:
You may resume driving when approved by your orthopedic surgeon. 
 We do not recommend driving while wearing a sling.

Return to Work: You may return to work as soon as you are 
comfortable.  This is dependent on your job type.  You will not 
be able to do any work with your arm for 6 weeks post-op.  You 
will not be able to return to work with any heavy lifting until 
___ months post-op.  Return to work notes can be obtained from 
your orthopedic surgeon at your first post-op appointment.

Antibiotics:
You need to take antibiotics 30 minutes prior to any dental 
procedure for the next ___ years following your Total Shoulder 
Replacement.  The prescription for this antibiotic will be given 
at your first post-operative appointment.  

Follow-up Appointment:
Your first post-operative appointment will be scheduled 10  14 
days following your surgical procedure. At this appointment your 
sutures will be removed.  If you do not have a post-operative 
appointment scheduled when you leave following surgery, please 
call ___ to make the appointment.  Your first 
appointment will be with a physician ___ (PA) or nurse 
practitioner (___).

Physical Therapy:
Non weight bearing, Passive ROM with <30 ER

Treatments Frequency:
any sutures that need to be removed will be addressed at the 2 
week followup appointment

worsening renal function, with creatinine increased to greater 
than 3. His only new medications are Januiva and glipizide since 
___, along with curcumin. He also reports several years of 
shortness of breath, with increasing dyspnea over the past ___ 
months. He denies any chest pain, palpitations, shortness of 
breath at rest. He also endorses on and off back pain for

has been going to physical therapy, with no real improvement in 
his back pain.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ year old man with DM2, CAD and CKD (baseline creatinine 1.8 
with recent worsening of unclear etiology) presents with 
worsening renal failure, found to be in heart block.  
.  
ACTIVE ISSUES:
# RHYTHM/ AV block: Pt has history of 1st degree AV block with 
complete heart block when he was on atenolol. His EKG in the ED 
on presentation showed AV dissociation with irregular R-R 
intervals consistent with high degree AV block. He had a carotid 
sinus massage which revealed slowing of his sinus rate but no 
change in A/V conduction. He then received 0.5mg atropine with 
increase in his sinus rate to ___, ventricular rates to ___ with 
Mobitz I block. His heart rate was also noted to increase with 
ambulation and minimal exertion. Because his increasing 
shortness of breath with minimal exertion was thought to be a 
symptom of his heart block, he had a pacemaker implanted with 
good results. He was discharged with Keflex for 48 hours.
.  
#Renal Failure: Pt initially presented to the ED at the urging 
of his nephrologist for 1 month of acutely worsening renal 
function. The progression of his disease was thought to be too 
fast to be due to progression of diabetic nephropathy but was 
rather attributed to his high degree AV block and poor forward 
flow. His urine lytes were consistent with pre-renal etiology. 
He had a renal ultrasound as an outpatient which was benign. His 
indapamide, furosemide and spironolactone were initially held 
but resumed per renal recommendations. His Diovan was also held 
initially, but resumed on discharge at lower dose. He will 
follow-up closely with renal now that he has had a pacemaker 
implantation with hopeful improvement in creatinine.
.
# CORONARIES: Pt was continued on his statin, aspirin, and 
Imdur. His Valsartan was initially held but resumed at lower 
dosage on discharge.   
.  
#Leukocytosis - Patient had a mild leukocytosis following 
pacemaker implantation, but no fevers, no readily-identifiable 
source of infection, other than renal failure. His UA was 
negative, urine culture negative, and CXR not concerning for 
acute process. Leukocytosis was attributed to inflammation from 
pacemaker implantation and should resolve quickly.  
.  
# HLD - continued statin.  
.  
# Hypertension - continued Imdur, decreased Valsartan due to 
renal failure.  
.  
# Diabetes Type 2: Pt has poorly controlled diabetes with last 
A1c 9.9% ___. He is currently enrolled in a study for 
geriatric DM control which was how his renal failure was picked 
up on.  He was continued on his home Lantus dose. His Januvia 
and Glipizide were held for his acute renal failure. These were 
resumed on discharge, though there was a question regarding his 
glipizide dose. He was discharged on dose consistent with last 
documented dose in ___ note, though this was different that 
what the patient reported taken. He was instructed to follow-up 
with the geriatric DM study directors for further 
recommendations on Glipizide dose.   
.  
# OSA - continued on CPAP.  
.  
# Gout - continued on home allopurinol renally dosed.  
.
TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
Pt should be followed closely in ___ clinic for improvement in 
his creatinine, as would be expected if his renal failure was in 
fact due to poor forward flow from high degree AV block now that 
he is s/p pacemaker implantation.
.
Pt should also follow up with the geriatric diabetes study 
coordinators as it was unclear what dose of glipizide he should 
have been discharged on.

Discharge Instructions:
PENILE PROSTHESIS                                                
                                Revised ___, ___ PA-C

-Please also refer to the instructions provided to you by the 
manufacturer of this prosthesis

- -AT least ONE to TWO times daily, GENTLY PULL THE BULB "DOWN" 
into the dependent scrotum (unless otherwise explicitly 
advised.)

-ALWAYS call to inform, review and discuss any medication 
changes and your post-operative course with your primary care 
doctor.

-over the next several days you may experience some increased 
swelling of your phallus (and scrotum) resembling a semi-rigid 
phallus (semi-erect); this is "normal" from the prosthesis and 
related surgery/edema. This may be accompanied by discoloration 
(ecchymosis) involving the phallus and the scrotum; this too is 
normal and will gradually resolve.

-Please remove the surgical dressing over penis and/or under 
scrotum on post-operative day two: no further wound care is 
needed and you may leave the wound open to air. 

-Please keep your phallus at midline, pointed toward your 
umbilicus, taped in place with protective gauze/pads (if 
necessary) for the next ___ days. Of course you may point it 
downward for voiding.

-Use a jock-supporter/strap or jockey-type briefs or tight, 
tighty-whities to facilitate this; Subsequently you may 
transition to loose fitting briefs or boxer-briefs for 
support--they should be cotton and/or breathable.

-Do NOT use prosthesis for 6 weeks and until explicitly advised 
by your urologist

-Complete a 7-day course of antibiotics as directed 

-You may shower, but do NOT bathe, swim or otherwise immerse 
your incision.

-Do NOT lift anything heavier than a phone book and no sports, 
vigorous physical activity (including sexual).

-Tylenol should be your first line pain medication, a narcotic 
pain medication has been prescribed for breakthough pain >4.   
The maximum daily Tylenol/Acetaminophen dose is ___ grams FROM 
ALL sources.

-Do NOT drive or drink alcohol while taking narcotics and do NOT 
operate dangerous machinery. 
For your safety, please do NOT DRIVE FOR ONE WEEK AFTER SURGERY 
or unless otherwise advised. 

-Colace has been prescribed to avoid post-surgical constipation 
and constipation related to narcotic pain medication, 
discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. Colace is a 
stool "softener"- it is NOT a laxative

-Resume your home medications, EXCEPT HOLD NSAIDs (ibuprofen 
containing products such as Advil & Motrin, Excedrin) for ONE 
week or until you see your urologist in follow-up OR you are 
explicitly advised to resume sooner by your PCP, ___ or 
Cardiologist.  DO NOT RESUME medications like VIAGRA, LEVITRA or 
CIALIS.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ was admitted to the ___ Spine Surgery Service on 
___ and taken to the Operating Room for C4-7 fusion through 
an anterior approach. Please refer to the dictated operative 
note for further details. The surgery was without complication 
and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable 
condition. TEDs/pnemoboots were used for postoperative DVT 
prophylaxis. Intravenous antibiotics were given per standard 
protocol. Initial postop pain was controlled with a PCA. On HD#2 
he returned to the operating room for a scheduled C4-7 
decompression with PSIF as part of a staged 2-part procedure. 
Please refer to the dictated operative note for further details. 
The second surgery was also without complication and the patient 
was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. Postoperative 
HCT was stable. On HD#3 he was taken for a posterior T4-11 
fusion with instrumentation.  Please see operative report for 
details. He was kept NPO until bowel function returned then diet 
was advanced as tolerated. His white count was elevated which 
was thought to be due to IV steroids.  He remained afebrile. The 
patient was transitioned to oral pain medication when tolerating 
PO diet. Foley was removed on POD#2 from the third procedure but 
had to be reinserted due to urinary retention of 1L.  He was 
fitted with a TLSO brace for ambulation. Physical therapy was 
consulted for mobilization OOB to ambulate. Hospital course was 
otherwise unremarkable. On the day of discharge the patient was 
afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain 
control and tolerating a regular diet.

Discharge Instructions:
You have undergone the following operation: ANTERIOR/POSTERIOR 
Cervical fusion with posterior thoracic fusion 

 
Immediately after the operation:
 

 
-Activity: You should not lift anything greater than 10 lbs for 
2 weeks. You will be more comfortable if you do not sit or stand 
more than ~45 minutes without getting up and walking around.
 

 
-Rehabilitation/ Physical Therapy: 
 
o2-3 times a day you should go for a walk for ___ minutes as 
part of your recovery. You can walk as much as you can tolerate. 

 
oLimit any kind of lifting.
 

 
-Diet: Eat a normal healthy diet. You may have some constipation 
after surgery. You have been given medication to help with this 
issue.
 

 
-Brace: You have been given a brace and a collar. These are to 
be worn when you are walking. You may take it off when sitting 
in a chair or while lying in bed.
 

 
-Wound Care: Remove the dressing in 2 days. If the incision is 
draining cover it with a new sterile dressing. If it is dry then 
you can leave the incision open to the air. Once the incision is 
completely dry (usually ___ days after the operation) you may 
take a shower. Do not soak the incision in a bath or pool. If 
the incision starts draining at anytime after surgery, do not 
get the incision wet. Cover it with a sterile dressing. Call the 
office. 
 

 
-You should resume taking your normal home medications. No 
NSAIDs.
 

 
-You have also been given Additional Medications to control your 
pain. Please allow 72 hours for refill of narcotic 
prescriptions, so please plan ahead. You can either have them 
mailed to your home or pick them up at the clinic located on 
___ 2. We are not allowed to call in or fax narcotic 
prescriptions (oxycontin, oxycodone, percocet) to your pharmacy. 
In addition, we are only allowed to write for pain medications 
for 90 days from the date of surgery.
 

 
Please call the office if you have a fever>101.5 degrees 
Fahrenheit and/or drainage from your wound.

.
#ASEPTIC MENINGITIS: Following LP, Mr. ___ was started on 
broad coverage with meningitic doses of vanc, ceftriaxone, 
ampicillin, and acyclovir for 48 hours given suspicion for 
meningitis and ID and Neurology were both consulted. After 48 
hours, Mr. ___ still reported sweats and fevers, CSF studies 
were consistent with aseptic meningitis, and initial culture 
results were negative so his antibiotics were discontinued and 
he was started on doxycycline for possible ehrlichia infection 
given his consititutional symptoms, transaminitis, sudden 
leukopenia, and history of frequent outdoor exposure and 
gardening. CSF crypto antigen test, viral culture, and 
bacterial/fungal cultures were negative. Blood and urine 
cultures were also negative and serum lyme titers were negative. 
Serum titers for CMV and EBV were negative for IgM and positive 
for IgG suggesting remote exposure/infection. Brain, C-spine, 
and ___ MRI's were all negative. A number of other CSF 
studies were suggested by the ID team yet there was limited 
fluid available in the lab and it was deemed that the benefits 
of further testing were outweighed by the risks and discomfort 
associated with an additional LP. Mr. ___ improved rapidly 
following initiation of therapy for suspected ehrlichia 
infection and was discharged on a 10 day course with close 
follow-up established with both his PCP and infectious disease 
doctor.
. 
#HIV: Mr. ___ presented as a chronic HIV positive patient 
without ever being on HAART. His CD4 count on admission in his 
relatively leukopenic state was 114. The possible need for 
initiation of pharmacologic therapy was addressed with him as an 
inpatient and he seemed agreeable to the idea of initiating 
therapy following discharge from the hospital. He was started on 
Bactrim prophylaxis on discharge and follow-up appointments were 
established with both his PCP and ID specialist.

___ is an ___ yo male with adeno ca of the RML who chose to
undergo therapy with cyberknife however it was not possible for 
technical reasons. He generally feels well, denies cough,

does admit to 20 lb weight loss over the last tear. He presents

Brief Hospital Course:
___ was admitted to the hospital and taken to the Operating 
Room where he underwent a robotic, converted to the open, right 
middle lobectomy and mediastinal lymph node dissection. He 
tolerated the procedure well and returned to the PACU in stable 
condition.  He maintained stable hemodynamics and his pain was 
minimal.  His chest tube drained minimally and was without air 
leak.

Following transfer to the Surgical floor he had his chest tube 
removed on ___ and his post pull chest xray showed a tiny 
right apical space.  He had some difficulty with urinary 
retention requiring replacement of his Foley catheter and he was 
eventually able to void after a second voiding trial.  He had a 
few episodes of confusion which were self limiting and his 
narcotics were stopped which helped.  His pain was controlled 
with scheduled Tylenol and an occasional Tramadol.  He was able 
to cough and deep breath and use his incentive spirometer +/- 
effectively.  His right thoracotomy site was healing well.

The Physical Therapy service evaluated ___ to assure his 
safety in walking independently and due to instability 
recommended his admission to an acute rehabilitation facility

Discharge Instructions:
* You were admitted to the hospital for lung surgery and you've 
recovered well. You are now ready for discharge but will need to 
spend some time at rehab prior to returning home.
* Continue to use your incentive spirometer 10 times an hour 
while awake.
* Check your incisions daily and report any increased redness or 
drainage.   Cover the area with a gauze pad if it is draining.
* Your chest tube dressing may be removed in 48 hours.  If it 
starts to drain, cover it with a clean dry dressing and change 
it  as needed to keep site clean and dry.
* You will continue to need pain medication once you are home 
but you can wean it over a few weeks as the discomfort resolves. 
 Make sure that you have regular bowel movements while on 
narcotic pain medications as they are constipating which can 
cause more problems.  Use a stool softener or gentle laxative to 
stay regular.
* Take Tylenol  ___ mg every 6 hours and use Tramadol for pain 
unrelieved by Tylenol.
* Continue to stay well hydrated and eat well to heal your 
incisions
* Shower daily. Wash incision with mild soap & water, rinse, pat 
dry
* No tub bathing, swimming or hot tubs until incision healed
* No lotions or creams to incision site
* Walk ___ times a day and gradually increase your activity as 
you can tolerate.

Call Dr. ___ ___ if you experience:
-Fevers > 101 or chills
-Increased shortness of breath, chest pain or any other symptoms 
that concern you.

Name:  ___                      Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: MEDICINE
 
Allergies: 
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
Dizziness, fevers/chills.
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None.
 
History of Present Illness:
Patient is a ___ M with PMHx of HTN, HLD, Diabetes, 
hypothyrodisim, and mild dementia who presents to the ED feeling 
fatigued for ___ days and with fevers.  
Patient reports that he has been experiencing dizziness that 
started about 4 days ago. He was shopping with his stepson when 
he noticed that he could not keep up and then felt dizziness and 
weak in the legs. He reports that he has had no other symptoms 
of dizziness since. He had fevers that started 1 week ago that 
were not daily. HE says that his last fever was ___ days ago. He 
himself did not check his temperature, but his wife did. He is 
unable to remember the values, but per report from the ED, the 
patient had a fever 2 days ago measured at 102 and a fever the 
night prior to admission measured at 101. Per report, there were 
associated chills/rigors and sweats. Per report, he was feeling 
well prior and reports that he currently feels well. The patient 
denies headaches/vision changes, neck stiffness, confusion, no 
cough/runny nose, no SOB/CP, no abdominal pain, no N/V, no 
diarrhea. No recent skin breaks/trauma. He denies sick contacts. 
He states that he needs to get dental work done. He reports that 
he checks his feet for ulcers.  
 
In the ED, initial VS were: 96.8 85 117/55 16 98%. Labs were 
notable for WBC 7.1 with 70.5 % PMNs, HCT 41.3, Creatinine 1.5, 
with BUN 27. CXR showing a 2 cm focal opacity within the right 
lung base was not clearly identified on the prior study. Patient 
received 1gram IV vancomycin because there was concern that he

Brief Hospital Course:
BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE
===============
___ is an ___ year old gentleman with history of 
hypertension and diabetes who presented with chronic malaise, 
weakness, fatigue, recent fevers, without localizing symptoms. 
He remained afebrile in the hospital with improved symptoms, but 
was noted to have new opacity on CXR. His CT chest was 
concerning for malignant focus, as such he was referred to 
interventional pulmonology team for follow-up bronchoscopy as an 
outpatient.

ACTIVE ISSUES
=========
# RML pulmonary nodule: Differential could include atypical 
infectious process, scarring, inflammation, or malignancy.  He 
has had recent weight loss, malaise, fatigue, and no localizing 
symptoms besides rhinorrhea. Given imaging and symptoms 
concerning for malignancy, he was referred to the interventional 
pulmonary team for outpatient bronchoscopy with biopsies in 
order to get a diagnosis. We discussed the possibilities with 
___ and his wife, and they were understanding.

# Fevers/chills at home: As noted above, he did not have any 
localizing symptoms for infection besides poor dentition and 
rhinorrhea, which he reported was chronic and allergic in 
nature. He was afebrile during admission. He may have had a 
recent viral upper respiratory tract infection. Fevers and 
malaise could also be reactive due to malignancy, but high 
temperatures make this less likely. Though he had poor 
dentition, he had no signs of active infection or abscess. He 
did get antibiotics in the emergency department, but these were 
not continued on the floor.
- Blood and urine cultures have been finalized as negative.

# Acute on chronic kidney injury: Creatinine baseline has been 
1.3-1.5, improved with IVF to 1.2. He did not get a dye load for 
his chest CT. 

CHRONIC ISSUES
==========
# Hypertension: Continued lisinopril.  His metoprolol and 
nifedipine were converted to short acting agents temporarily 
while in house. Home medications re-started at discharge.

# Diabetes mellitus:  Metformin was held on admission given his 
acute kidney injury. He was managed with a humalog insulin 
sliding scale. Given improved kidney function with hydration, 
his metformin was re-started on discharge.
- Consider discontinuing metformin if creatinine worsens or 
fluctuates a great deal.

# Hyperlipidemia: Continued home statin.  

# Dementia: Slightly confused at baseline but very attentive (on 
second morning of admission, could not recall where he was, or 
recognize his medical team, but was easily re-oriented). 
Important discussions were held while his wife was also present. 
 He was continued on his home donepezil.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
==============
- Code status: Full code, but wants to discuss with his wife.
- Emergency contact: ___, daughter, ___, ___ 
- Studies pending on discharge: All cultures that were pending 
have been finalized without growth.
- Consider discontinuing metformin if creatinine worsens or 
fluctuates a great deal.
- Please follow up his pathology and bronchoscopy results 
(scheduled for ___.
- Calcium and TSH pending on discharge, please follow these up.

Brief Hospital Course:
#  Sinus Infection: CT head shows maxillary sinus disease 
consistent with patient's exam. Her current headache is also 
similar to prior sinus headaches (although her BP is likely also 
contributing). Her other symptoms including sore throat and 
cough are also consistent with acute sinus infection. It has 
only been going on for about 1 week so there is no indication 
for antibiotics as more likely a viral infection at this time. 
Treated symptomatically with afrin X 3 days, flonase, tylenol, 
oxycodone PRN at low dose for headache and morphine if needed 
for breakthrough pain. Also guaifensin for cough. At the time of 
discharge the patient felt much better.

#  HTN: Likely worsened in setting of sinus infection. No signs 
of hypertensive emergency on admission and although headache is 
recurring her BP is in much better control so headache may not 
be ___ to just HTN. Her BP was initially brought down acutely in 
the ___ with morphine and metoprolol. On admission to the floor 
she was started on her home dose of diovan which controlled her 
blood pressures and no further medications needed to be added.

# Headache: Likely ___ her sinus infection and exacerbated by 
HTN. Doubt temporal arteritis given only mildly elevated ESR, no 
fevers, no jaw pain, and no temporal artery tenderness on exam. 
Also no visual changes. Was treated with tylenol, oxycodone, and 
morphine IV as above and BP control as well. On discharge this 
had resolved.
  
#  Dysphagia: although after gathering more history this is more 
likely related to the sore throat from her sinus infection she 
was witnessed to have choking in ___ so obtained speech and 
swallow eval here. Their recommendations were to put patient on 
a pureed diet and thin liquids. They also recommended an ENT and 
GI consult. We have deferred this workup to the outpatient 
setting as the patient was feeling better on discharge. If she 
continues to have difficulty she will follow up with ENT and 
speech and swallow for a video swallow. 

# Mediastinal LAD: Patient had slightly enlarged mediastinal LNs 
on CXR than they were previously and with the hoarse voice and 
difficulty swallowing concerned that she could have some nerve 
impingement. She underwent CT chest which showed that the lymph 
nodes were calcified but actually smaller than her previous 
scan. No further imaging was suggested.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ is a ___ year old ___ woman with pulmonary sarcoidosis 
and HTN who presented to the BI ED with right-sided facial 
paresthesia most consistent with TIA.  She was admitted to the 
stroke service for work-up. 
 
# NEURO: 
On initial examination, the patient demonstrated patchy sensory 
changes with right < left to temperature in all distributions 
and some blunting of the right nasolabial fold with expression 
of air on puffed cheek testing. She was put on ASA 325mg. ___ 
demonstrated no evidence for any acute intracranial process. CTA 
showed subtle left internal carotid artery beading, but this was 
not significant. MRI was negative for acute infarct but 
demonstrated mild small vessel ischemic disease. Approximately 
15 hours after onset, her symptoms resolved. On the day of 
discharge she had no focal neurologic deficits. Stroke risk 
factors were evaluated: HbA1c was 5.8%, lipid panel [pending at 
time of discharge], echo [no pfo or evidence of arrhythmia], 
nonsmoker. Based on the negative imaging and symptomatic course, 
her symptoms were thought to be due to a transient ischemic 
attack. She was discharged on ASA 81mg and given stroke 
information packet.
 
# ___:
While the patient was admitted, her stroke risk factors were 
evaluated (HbA1c 5.8%, LDL pending). She demonstrated no 
evidence of arrythmias. Her TTE with bubble was negative. Her BP 
was allowed to autoregulate x 20 hours and then she was 
restarted on her home hypertensives: losartan and amlodipine.  
Please note that patient may actually be taking her losartan and 
amlodipine at wrong dosages and this will be addressed by PCP 
and PACT on outpatient basis.  Patient counseled on correct 
doses.

# PULM: 
Patient has a known history of sarcoidosis, never systemically 
treated. She received a CXR during admission which was 
consistent with sarcoidosis, no acute findings.  Breathing on 
RA.
 
# ENDO:
HbA1c value of 5.8%. 
 
# TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
1. Patient stated that she takes her amlodipine BID and losartan 
daily, the opposite of what is prescribed. Patient has been 
enrolled in the PACT program. PACT alerted PCP.
2. At time of discharge her lipid panel is pending. Pending 
results she may need to be started on a statin. 
3. She will follow up with Dr. ___ in clinic.

___ at a time. On exam, patient had L eye hypertropia, 
with the axis of patients R eye sitting lower than her L eye 
axis. Patients exam was concerning for involvement of multiple 
CN's, with concern for muscle twitching around orbicularis oculi 
to full hemifacial spasm affecting CNVII, as well as L eye 
hypertropia effecting cranial nerves involved with EOM. Due to 
patients known history of sarcoid, concern remained for sarcoid 
affecting multiple CN's versus a granuloma compression on the 
facial nerve. Patient was thus admitted for further workup 
including a stroke workup. 

Patient thus underwent stroke r/o including CT/CTA and MRI which 
showed no hemorrhage or infarct. 

Furthermore, patients imaging was notable for a 3mm aneurysm of 
the supraclinoid segment of the right ICA, as well as a 1 mm 
outpouching of the communicating segment of the right ICA, 
representing either infundibulum or tiny aneurysm. Given her 
age, as well as small diameter and location of this 3mm right 
supraclinoid ICA aneurysm, this can be followed as an 
outpatient. Patient otherwise had patent intracranial arterial 
vasculature without significant stenosis, or occlusion, with 
mild atherosclerosis. Furthermore a mild beaded appearance of 
the bilateral internal carotid arteries, was appreciated, which 
can be seen in the setting of fibromuscular dysplasia. 
Furthermore, MRI was obtained which showed no evidence of acute 
infarction or other diffusion abnormality, however showed FLAIR 
and T2 hyperintense foci in the periventricular and subcortical 
white matter and pons which were nonspecific but could be 
secondary to chronic small vessel ischemic disease, unchanged 
from evaluation in ___. Stroke risk factor workup was largely 
negative with HgbA1c of 5.6, LDLcalc of 85 and TSH of 1.1. 

An MRI w/ and w/o contrast with thin cuts through the brainstem 
was declined by patient. Additionally, lumbar puncture (to look 
for evidence of neurosarcoidosis) was declined. Recommended 
Carbamezapine for treatment of hemifacial spasm but patient 
declined due to possible side effects. She will follow-up with 
Dr. ___ in stroke clinic and make an appointment to see the 
Movement disorders specialists for consideration of Botox 
therapy if the right hemifacial spasm continues.

You presented to the hospital due to symptoms of R face muscle 
twitching and double vision. You were evaluated in the ED with 
labs and a CT/CTA of the head and neck which showed no evidence 
of a hemorrhage or acute ischemic stroke.

hemifacial spasm as well as concern over your reported double 
vision you were admitted to the inpatient neurology service. 
During your hospitalization, a MRI of the brain was obtained 
which showed no evidence of an ischemic stroke. Furthermore, CTA 
of the vessels of your brain head showed a stable 3mm aneurysm 
which looks similar in size to your last MRI in ___, with no 
evidence of occlusion or narrowing of the blood vessels of your 
brain. 

We think your symptoms are from hemifacial spasm due to possible 
compression of the right facial nerve. It is possible that this 
may be also related to your sarcoidosis. We recommended further 
testing with a more in depth MRI and lumbar puncture, but you 
declined this. 

We recommended Carbamezepine for treatment of your facial spasm 
but you declined. We will give you the number for the movement 
disorders clinic to set up an appointment to be evaluated. 

Please take your medications as prescribed.

Please follow up with Neurology and your primary care physician 
as listed below.

If you experience any of the symptoms below, please seek 
emergency medical attention by calling Emergency Medical 
Services (dialing 911). In particular, since stroke can recur, 
please pay attention to the sudden onset and persistence of 
these symptoms:
- Sudden partial or complete loss of vision
- Sudden loss of the ability to speak words from your mouth
- Sudden loss of the ability to understand others speaking to 
you
- Sudden weakness of one side of the body
- Sudden drooping of one side of the face
- Sudden loss of sensation of one side of the body

Your ___ Neurology Team

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
====================
Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with hypertension and 
sarcoidosis who presented to the ED with 10 days of productive 
cough, malaise, and subjective fevers/chills. Her CXR was 
unchanged from prior and could not rule out a consolidation, so 
she was admitted for community acquired pneumonia treatment. 

ACUTE ISSUES:
=============

# CAP
The patient presented with cough, low grade temperatures, and 
shortness of breath. She reports 10 days of malaise and cough, 
with 4 days of yellow-green sputum. Her symptoms were most 
likely viral in origin. A CXR showed possible underlying 
consolidation, however this was difficult to discern given her 
underlying sarcoidosis and hilar adenopathy. Her Flu swab was 
negative. Her CURB-65 score was 1 (for age only). She received 
Dextromethorphan-Guaifenesin q6h PRN as well as Benzonatate TID 
PRN cough. She was continued on Ceftriaxone and Azithromycin 
which were converted to PO azithromycin and PO Augmentin given 
that she had no hypoxia and a reassuring exam. On the day of 
discharge she walked without issues and had no episodes of 
hypoxia. She reported feeling back to her baseline. She was 
discharged with close PCP follow up. 

CHRONIC ISSUES:
===============
# Sarcoidosis
CXR on admission showed: "peribronchovascular diffuse 
ill-defined opacities are slightly more pronounced when compared 
with prior chest radiograph and may reflect progression of 
patient's known sarcoidosis". 

# Leg pain
Chronic, secondary to neuropathy vs sciatica per outpatient 
notes. She was continued on her home gabapentin 600-900mg QHS 
PRN. 

# HTN
- Continued home amlodipine 2.5mg qd

# HLD
- Continued home atorvastatin 10mg QHS

# Allergic rhintiis
- Continued home intranasal fluticasone qd

had no fevers or leukocytosis.  She was given hydration and 
maintained on a clear liquid diet intially.  She was finally 
advanced to a regular diet prior to discharge and tolerated this 
well.

breath, more with exertion.  Denied chest pain or palpitation, 
___ edema, orthopnea, or PND.  Her BNP was wnl and her EKG 
initially showed a great deal of artifact, but repeat was normal 
sinus rhythm without ischemic changes.  She had negative 
troponins x 2.  Her CXR revealed hilar adenopathy consistent 
with her underlying sarcoid and also showed interstitial 
disease, likely contributing to her shortness of breath.  She 
maintained adequate oxygen saturations, but her symptoms should 
be followed as an outpatient.

neck and into her L arm.  She had no associated symptoms of 
chest pain, nausea, emesis, lightheadedness, diaphoresis or 
shortness of breath with her shoulder pain.  She had full range 
of motion on exam, but with significant pain at the extremes of 
movement.  She was given morphine in the hospital with relief 
and also received a lidoderm patch with improvement in her 
symptoms.  She was given a prescription for lidoderm patch on 
discharge to see if this would improve her pain control at home 
in addition to tylenol.

dose.

outpatient follow up.

- inactive

to

___ ankle: Intraoperative images were obtained during ORIF of
the right talar fracture without radiologist present.   

___ ___ 1. Severely comminuted talar body/neck fracture with
talonavicular and subtalar intra-articular extensions.  Multiple
displaced fracture fragments, with a large fragment in the sinus
tarsi. 2. Minimally displaced sustentaculum tali calcaneal
fracture with subtalar intra-articular extension. 3. 
Nondisplaced
anterolateral distal tibial cortical fracture, representing a
capsular avulsion injury.  Multiple tiny fracture fragments in
the tibiotalar joint space. 4. Tiny displaced fracture fragments
adjacent to medial malleolus, raises

fracture fragment adjacent to lateral malleolus, likely 
secondary to direct injury. 6. Diffuse soft tissue swelling.

___ b/l foot: 1. Suspicion for a minimally displaced fracture
involving the head of the left fifth metatarsal. 
2. Re-demonstration of a fracture through the neck of the right

___ L wrist: No acute fractures or dislocation are seen. There

___ CT face: 1. Comminuted and displaced fractures of the
bilateral nasal bones and frontal processes of the maxilla
bilaterally. 2. Extraluminal air and debris is seen within the
fascial planes of the neck including the retropharyngeal and
"danger space" . This may represent the 
sequelae of esophageal tearing, tracheal disruption or injury

___ CT head:  1. No acute intracranial process. 
2. Comminuted, displaced fractures of the bilateral nasal bones
with involvement of the frontal process of the right maxilla. 
3. Paranasal sinus disease, a component of which may be reactive

___ CT chest/pelvis: 1. Extraluminal air and debris is seen

___ CT c-spine: 1. No evidence of traumatic malalignment or

the
fascial planes of the neck including the retropharyngeal space
and "danger space" . This may represent the sequelae of
esophageal tearing, tracheal disruption or injury within the 
oropharynx (2:54, 68, 80). 3. A curvilinear focus of air 
adjacent
to the left lateral margin of the esophagus is seen in the very
superior mediastinum (601:37). 4. There is soft tissue stranding

___ CXR:No evidence of focal consolidation, large pleural

normal in appearance.  Retropharyngeal air, better seen on
concurrently performed CT. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ is a ___ yo M who presented to the Emergency 
Department on ___ after a motor vehicle crash. He was 
hemodynamically stable reporting bilateral ankle pain. The 
patient was found to have extraluminal air and debris in the 
fascial planes of the neck including in the retropharyngeal 
space which is concerning for a possible esophageal tear or 
tracheal disruption. ENT was consulted. The patient had a stable 
exam and underwent awake bronchoscopy by anesthesia team, 
esophagoscopy, placement of NG tube under direct visualization, 
and bronchoscopy. No injuries were identified. Plastic surgery 
sutured lacerations in the face and evaluated facial fractures. 
Sinus precautions were recommended and outpatient follow up. 
Orthopedic surgery was consulted for the right talar neck highly 
comminuted fracture and the patient underwent ORIF on ___ 
with a cast then placed on ___. The patient was admitted to 
the Trauma service for ongoing pain management and monitoring.

Pain was initially managed with IV hydromorphone and 
acetaminophen and was then transitioned to oral once tolerating 
a regular diet. On HD2 nasogastric tube was removed and he was 
given a clear liquid diet without difficulty. On HD3 he 
underwent surgery with orthopedics for comminuted right talar 
fracture. Tertiary survey was done which revealed a left fifth 
metatarsal fracture. Orthopedics was made aware and recommended 
a hard sole shoe. Post operatively, diet was advanced to regular 
which was tolerated well. On HD7 plastic surgery removed facial 
sutures. He was seen and evaluated by physical therapy who 
recommended rehab but he was ultimately making progress and 
cleared for discharge home with home ___. 

The patient voided without problem. During this hospitalization, 
the patient ambulated early and frequently with assist, was 
adherent with respiratory toilet and incentive spirometry, and 
actively participated in the plan of care. The patient received 
subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during this 
stay.

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, getting out of bed and mobilizing with assistance, voiding 
without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient 
was discharged home with services. The patient and his spouse 
received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with 
understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.

and frontal processes of the maxilla bilaterally.

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.

Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker 
or cane).

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___,

You were admitted to the Acute Care Surgery Service on ___ 
after a motor vehicle crash with multiple injuries including air 
in the soft tissues of your neck, facial lacerations and 
fractures, a right ankle (Talar bone) fracture, and a left 
metatarsal bone (top of foot) fracture. 

You underwent an endoscopy of your trachea and esophagus by the 
Acute Care Surgery team and there was no injury identified. The 
otolaryngology team was consulted and you underwent direct 
visualization with a camera into your neck and lungs. No 
injuries were identified. You underwent surgery with the 
orthopedic service to repair your ankle fracture. You were seen 
and evaluated by the plastic surgery team who sutured your 
facial lacerations and recommended outpatient follow up to see 
if your nasal bone fracture needs surgery. Please continue to 
follow sinus precautions until ___ (elevate head on pillows, 
no smoking, no nose blowing, open mouth sneezing, no drinking 
through straws.) You were seen and evaluated by physical therapy 
who recommends that you be discharged to home with physical 
therapy services.

You are now doing better, tolerating a regular diet, and pain is 
better controlled on oral medications.  Please follow the 
discharge instructions below:

Regarding your Orthopedic Injuries:

-Do not bear weight on your right leg.  You may touchdown on the 
right heel.  You may bear weight as tolerated on the left leg 
while wearing a hard-soled shoe. 

Please call your doctor or nurse practitioner or return to the 
Emergency Department for any of the following:
*You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or 
tightness.
*New or worsening cough, shortness of breath, or wheeze.
*If you are vomiting and cannot keep down fluids or your 
medications.
*You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting, diarrhea, 
or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry mouth, rapid 
heartbeat, or feeling dizzy or faint when standing.
*You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a 
bowel movement.
*You experience burning when you urinate, have blood in your 
urine, or experience a discharge.
*Your pain in not improving within ___ hours or is not gone 
within 24 hours. Call or return immediately if your pain is 
getting worse or changes location or moving to your chest or 
back.
*You have shaking chills, or fever greater than 101.5 degrees 
Fahrenheit or 38 degrees Celsius.
*Any change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that concern 
you.

Please resume all regular home medications, unless specifically 
advised not to take a particular medication. Also, please take

appointment.

History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is a healthy ___ year old male with a history of 
internal hemorrhoids status post banding (___) and 
colonoscopy / endoscopy (___) and iron deficiency anemia 
(baseline 23 - 28 (see below in ___)) who presents with bright 
red blood per rectum, dyspnea on exertion, and anemia. He was 
feeling increased fatigue and dysnpea over the last ___ weeks; 
yesterday his PCP found him to have a low hematocrit and sent 
him to our ED.

Over the past ___ weeks he endorses worsening dyspnea with 
exertion, lightheadedness, dizziness, fatigue; he had difficulty 
exercising and going up stairs.

He reports that he has had internal hemorrhoids for ___ years 
and the bleeding is worst in times of stress when he has changes 
to his sleep and eating patterns. Over the past 6 months, the 
bleeding has increased in frequency to occur during every bowel 
movement; he endorses ___ bowel movements per day.  He describes 
the blood as bright red and "like a squirt gun" and he endorses 
that it only stops if he applies pressure.  It is both in the 
toilet and on the toilet paper. He denied abdominal pain, 
nausea, vomiting, constipation or melena.

Of note, he has a family history of inflammatory bowel disease 
(unknown type).  His grandfather died of colon cancer at age ___.

Also of note, he endorses up to 12 cans of soda / day when he is 
stressed but is currently only drinking 2 cans per day. Also 
drinks 1 coffee per day. No smoking.  Alcohol use of 12 drinks 
per week.

His PCP has started him on iron supplementation in the past but 
he is unable to take the iron because of nausea and some 
vomiting with the pills. He also endorsed increased "motion 
sickness" when he is in a taxi or on the T. He denied any 
history of panic attacks.

In the ED his initial vitals were 97.8 84 128/67 18 100%. He was 
given 1L NS. A rectal exam revealed bright red blood with 
anoscopy showing an internal hemorroid that was not actively 
bleeding. He was crossed for two units. His transfer vitals were 
98.2, 71, 23, 116/56, 100%Ra.  

On the floor he was in no distress. He is NPO until GI consult.

Brief Hospital Course:
BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE: Mr. ___ is a healthy ___ year old male 
with a history of internal hemorrhoids status post banding and 
iron deficiency anemia who presents with bright red blood per 
rectum, dyspnea on exertion, and anemia.  Labs showed iron 
deficiency with a ferritin of 3.7.  He was given 1 unit of 
blood.  A anoscopy was performed by colorectal surgeons which 
did not reveal bleeding internal hemorrhoids.  A colonoscopy was 
performed while inpatient which revealed non-bleeding grade 2 
internal hemorrhoids and bleeding rectal ulcers.

# GI bleed: Mr. ___ has a ___ year history of intermittent 
BRBPR which has worsened over the past 6 months.  Over the past 
6 months, he has had persistent BRBPR which occur with bowel 
movements. He has ___ bowel movements per day with loose stool.  
He has internal hemorrhoids which were banded ___.  A past 
colonoscopy ___ showed internal hemorrhoids; a past 
endoscopy showed gastritis.  He was hemodynamically stable with 
no tachycardia and no hypotension. Given his symptomatic anemia 
he was given 1 u of packed RBCs.  Stool cultures were negative. 
HIV test was negative.  TTG and IgA were normal. Colorectal 
surgery did an anoscopy and did not see active bleeding so 
banding can occur on an outpatient basis. A colonoscopy was 
performed.  Colonoscopy showed internal hemorrhoids and bleeding 
rectal ulcers and diverticulosis of the cecum.  Biopsy results 
were taken and will be followed up by gastroenterology.  He will 
follow up with gastroenterology and colorectal surgery for 
further evaluation and management.

# ANEMIA: Mr. ___ HCT has been ___ over the past 8 months 
with one increase to 33 (___).  He reported reduced exercise 
capacity and was symptomatic with fatigue, dyspnea on exertion. 
Physical exam revealed conjunctival and sublingual pallor.  He 
received 1 unit PRBCs; HCT was stable and uptrending to 27. 
Reticulocytes were 1.7 with a reticulocyte index of 0.64.  B12 
was normal.  Iron studies were sent showing low ferritin 
suggesting iron deficient anemia.  The most likely cause is from 
the chronic blood loss in his GI tract.  Prior iron 
supplementation therapy (ferrous sulfate 325 TID) were 
unsuccessful because he was unable to tolerate the nausea side 
effects.  Ferrous sulfate was stopped and Ferrous Gluconate 325 
TID was started which may be better tolerated. He was counseled 
that there are several other therapies to try if he has side 
effects from the Ferrous gluconate. Prior to discharge, he was 
able to climb 3 flights of stairs without symptoms. 

INACTIVE ISSUES:
#Depression:  Stable, sees a therapist.  On the floor he denied 
suicide or homicide ideation.  We continued him oh his home 
fluoxetine 50 mg daily in the AM.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
# f/u TTG and IgA, stool cultures: GI appointment in 2 weeks- 
they will inform him of these normal results.
# f/u colon biopsies: GI appointment in 2 weeks and will inform 
him of these results.
# Colorectal and GI appointments in 2 weeks for follow up

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted on ___ under the acute care surgery 
service for management of her acute appendicitis. She was taken 
to the operating room and underwent a laparoscopic appendectomy. 
Please see operative report for details of this procedure. She 
tolerated the procedure well and was extubated upon completion. 
She was subsequently taken to the PACU for recovery.
She was transferred to the surgical floor hemodynamically 
stable. Her vital signs were routinely monitored and she 
remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable. She was initially 
given IV fluids postoperatively, which were discontinued when 
she was tolerating PO's. His diet was advanced on the morning of 
___ to regular, which she tolerated without abdominal pain, 
nausea, or vomiting. She was voiding adequate amounts of urine 
without difficulty. She was encouraged to mobilize out of bed 
and ambulate as tolerated, which she was able to do 
independently. Her pain level was routinely assessed and well 
controlled at discharge with an oral regimen as needed.
On ___, she was discharged home with instructions to follow 
up in ___ clinic in 2 weeks, a phone number was given.

 
Medications on Admission:
Flovent, pravastatin, ventolin, escitalopram,
sumatriptan  

 
Discharge Medications:
1. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release)  ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain 
RX *oxycodone 5 mg ___ tablet(s) by mouth every ___ hours Disp 
#*30 Tablet Refills:*0
2. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 
3. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain 

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
acute appendicitis

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital with acute appendicitis. You 
were taken to the operating room and had your appendix removed 
laparoscopically.  You tolerated the procedure well and are now 
being discharged home with the following instructions:
Please follow up at the appointment in clinic listed below. We 
also generally recommend that patients follow up with their 
primary care provider after having surgery. We have scheduled an 
appointment for you listed below.
ACTIVITY:
 
Do not drive until you have stopped taking pain medicine and 
feel you could respond in an emergency.
You may climb stairs. 
You may go outside, but avoid traveling long distances until you 
see your surgeon at your next visit.
Don't lift more than ___ lbs for ___ weeks. (This is about the 
weight of a briefcase or a bag of groceries.) This applies to 
lifting children, but they may sit on your lap.
You may start some light exercise when you feel comfortable.
You will need to stay out of bathtubs or swimming pools for a 
time while your incision is healing. Ask your doctor when you 
can resume tub baths or swimming.
 
HOW YOU MAY FEEL: 
You may feel weak or "washed out" a couple weeks. You might want 
to nap often. Simple tasks may exhaust you.
You may have a sore throat because of a tube that was in your 
throat during surgery.
You could have a poor appetite for a couple days. Food may seem 
unappealing.
All of these feelings and reactions are normal and should go 
away in a short time.  If they do not, tell your surgeon.
 
YOUR INCISION:
Tomorrow you may shower and remove the gauzes over your 
incisions. Under these dressings you have small plastic bandages 
called steristrips. Do not remove steri-strips for 2 weeks. 
(These are the thin paper strips that might be on your 
incision.) But if they fall off before that that's okay.
Your incisions may be slightly red around the stitches. This is 
normal.
You may gently wash away dried material around your incision.
Avoid direct sun exposure to the incision area.
Do not use any ointments on the incision unless you were told 
otherwise.
You may see a small amount of clear or light red fluid staining 
your dressing or clothes. If the staining is severe, please call 
your surgeon.
You may shower. As noted above, ask your doctor when you may 
resume tub baths or swimming.
 
YOUR BOWELS:
Constipation is a common side effect of narcotic pain 
medicaitons. If needed, you may take a stool softener (such as 
Colace, one capsule) or gentle laxative (such as milk of 
magnesia, 1 tbs) twice a day. You can get both of these 
medicines without a prescription.
If you go 48 hours without a bowel movement, or have pain moving 
the bowels, call your surgeon. 
 
PAIN MANAGEMENT:
It is normal to feel some discomfort/pain following abdominal 
surgery. This pain is often described as "soreness". 
Your pain should get better day by day. If you find the pain is 
getting worse instead of better, please contact your surgeon.
You will receive a prescription for pain medicine to take by 
mouth. It is important to take this medicine as directed. Do not 
take it more frequently than prescribed. Do not take more 
medicine at one time than prescribed. Do not drink alcohol or 
drive while taking narcotic pain medication.
Your pain medicine will work better if you take it before your 
pain gets too severe.
Talk with your surgeon about how long you will need to take 
prescription pain medicine. Please don't take any other pain 
medicine, including non-prescription pain medicine, unless your 
surgeon has said its okay.
If you are experiencing no pain, it is okay to skip a dose of 
pain medicine.
Remember to use your "cough pillow" for splinting when you cough 
or when you are doing your deep breathing exercises.
If you experience any of the following, please contact your 
surgeon:
- sharp pain or any severe pain that lasts several hours
- pain that is getting worse over time
- pain accompanied by fever of more than 101
- a drastic change in nature or quality of your pain
 
MEDICATIONS:
Take all the medicines you were on before the operation just as 
you did before, unless you have been told differently.
If you have any questions about what medicine to take or not to 
take, please call your surgeon.
 
DANGER SIGNS:
Please call your surgeon if you develop:
- worsening abdominal pain
- sharp or severe pain that lasts several hours
- temperature of 101 degrees or higher
- severe diarrhea
- vomiting
- redness around the incision that is spreading
- increased swelling around the incision
- excessive bruising around the incision
- cloudy fluid coming from the wound
- bright red blood or foul smelling discharge coming from the 
wound
- an increase in drainage from the wound

 
Followup Instructions:
___

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted to Urology with a RIGHT renal mass after 
undergoing robot-assisted laparoscopic right radical 
nephrectomy. No concerning intraoperative events occurred; 
please see dictated operative note for details. The patient 
received perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis. The patient was 
transferred to the floor from the PACU in stable condition. On 
POD0, pain was well controlled on PCA, hydrated with intravenous 
fluids keeping urine output at over >30cc/hour.  She provided 
with pneumoboots and incentive spirometry for prophylaxis. On 
POD1, the patient was transitioned from PCA to oral medications 
with the advancement to clears diet. She poorly tolerated the 
narcotics which caused dizziness, nausea and a general "unwell" 
feeling. She did not have emesis but did experience periorid 
nausea. This limited her cooperation with early mobilization and 
deep inspiration, incentive spirometry use. She is known to have 
obstructive sleep apnea and was maintained on telemetry for 
continuous oxygen saturation monitoring and provided CPAP and 
nasal canula use for overnight. She was given additional pain 
medications and anti-emetics and subsequently ambulated and 
advanced to clears with toast and crackers. She was not given 
Toradol because of her POD1 creatinine. At her request her 
narcotics were adjusted. Basic metabolic panel and complete 
blood count were checked daily. On POD2 her pain was moderately 
well controlled ranging from ___. Ms. ___ ambulated and 
continued to use the incentive spirometer. She was able to 
ambulate on room air without requirement of supplemental oxygen 
to keep her saturation greater than 92%. She did not feel well 
enough to go home and remained overnight. Diet was advanced as 
tolerated. Overnight on POD2 to POD3 she remained on nasal 
canula in lieu of CPAP mask/mouth-guard. On the morning of POD3 
she reported some increased discomfort with deep inspiration and 
a sense of shortness of breath. Her vitals though were stable 
and she was not tachypneic.  EKG and CXR obtained.  His pain 
medications were again adjusted and with improved serum 
creatinine, she was given a Toradol and this markedly improved 
her pain.  On POD3 she was converted to all oral medications and 
her pain was well controlled with out 
further nausea or emesis. Foley catheter was removed (POD2) 
without difficulty and she was voiding independently. The 
remainder of the hospital course was relatively unremarkable. 
The patient was discharged in stable condition, eating well, 
ambulating independently, voiding without difficulty, and with 
pain control on oral analgesics. On exam, incision was clean, 
dry, and intact, with no evidence of 
hematoma collection or infection. The patient was given explicit 
instructions to follow-up in clinic with Dr. ___ and to call 
with any questions.

Discharge Instructions:
-Resume your pre-admission/home medications except as noted. 
ALWAYS call to inform, review and discuss any medication changes 
and your post-operative course with your primary care doctor.

-___ reduce the strain/pressure on your abdomen and incision 
sites; remember to log roll onto your side and then use your 
hands to push yourself upright while taking advantage of the 
momentum of putting your legs/feet to the ground.

--There may be bandage strips called steristrips which have 
been applied to reinforce wound closure.  Allow these bandage 
strips to fall off on their own over time but PLEASE REMOVE ANY 
REMAINING GAUZE DRESSINGS WITHIN 2 DAYS OF DISCHARGE. You may 
get the steristrips wet.

-Please AVOID aspirin or aspirin containing products and 
supplements that may have blood-thinning effects (like Fish 
Oil, Vitamin E, etc.) unless you have otherwise been advised.

-IBUPROFEN (the ingredient of Advil, Motrin, etc.) may be taken 
even though you may also be taking Tylenol/Acetaminophen. You 
may alternate these medications for pain control.  For pain 
control, try TYLENOL FIRST, then ibuprofen, and then take the 
narcotic pain medication as prescribed if additional pain relief 
is needed.

-Ibuprofen should always be taken with food. Please discontinue 
taking and notify your doctor should you develop blood in your 
stool (dark, tarry stools)

-Call your Urologist's office to schedule/confirm your follow-up 
appointment in 4 weeks AND if you have any questions.

-Do not eat constipating foods for ___ weeks, drink plenty of 
fluids to keep hydrated

-No vigorous physical activity or sports for 4 weeks or until 
otherwise advised. Light household chores/activity and leisurely 
walking/activity is OK and should be continued. Do NOT be a 
couch potato

-Tylenol should be your first-line pain medication. A narcotic 
pain medication has been prescribed for breakthrough pain ___. 
REPLACE the Tylenol with this narcotic pain medication if 
additional pain control is needed.

-Max daily Tylenol (acetaminophen) dose is 4 grams from ALL 
sources, note that narcotic pain medication also contains 
Tylenol

-Do not lift anything heavier than a phone book (10 pounds) or 
drive until you are seen by your Urologist in follow-up 

-You may shower normally but do NOT immerse your incisions or 
bathe

-Do not drive or drink alcohol while taking narcotics and do not 
operate dangerous machinery

-Colace has been prescribed to avoid post surgical constipation 
and constipation related to narcotic pain medication. 
Discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. Colace is a 
stool-softener, NOT a laxative

-If you have fevers > 101.5 F, vomiting, or increased redness, 
swelling, or discharge from your incision, call your doctor or 
go to the nearest emergency room.

Brief Hospital Course:
MICU Course:
Given large hct drop and hypotension in the ED, the patient was 
admitted to the MICU with plans for EGD by GI while in the unit. 
He was started on a PPI drip. He received a total of 4 units of 
pRBCs throughout the course of the night of admission. He 
underwent EGD on the morning following admission, which showed a 
duodenal ulcer. His hematocrit continued to trend up and he had 
no further episodes of bleeding. Prior to transfer to the 
medical floor, he was tolerating a PO diet and was switched to 
an IV PPI BID. H.pylori serologies were sent and should be 
followed-up as an outpatient.
.
He was transferred to the medical floor day 2 of admission. He 
continued to tolerate a regular diet well and was without pain 
or any other symptoms. PPI was changed to Protonix po 40mg BID. 
Stools were soft and brown. Cardiology was contacted re: 
patient's need for ASA. Pt has no symptoms of angina at rest or 
with exertion, a negative stress test in ___, is not diabetic, 
and has dyslipidemia that is controlled on 20mg of simvistatin. 
Thus, the risk of continuing ASA outweighs the benefit in this 
case. He will be on po PPI BID for ___ weeks per GI recs. At 
that time, he will f/u with his PMD, review the results of H 
pylori serology and give triple therapy if appropriate, and 
discuss need for PPI long term. If it is determined that patient 
go back on ASA, he should also stay on a daily PPI.

Discharge Instructions:
It was a pleasure taking care of you at ___ 
___. You were admitted to the hospital because you were 
losing blood through your GI tract. The bleeding was found to be 
due to an ulcer in the duodenum. Your blood count was low, so we 
gave you 4 units of blood and it improved. You were started on a 
medicine called Protonix (pantoprazole) to help your GI tract 
heal. You had a test to look for a bacteria, H pylori, that can 
cause ulcers. When you see primary care provider, he or she will 
review those results with you. 

We made the following changes to your medicine:
1) Please STOP taking aspirin 81mg daily until told otherwise by 
your gastroenterologist
2) START taking Protonix 40mg twice a day. 
3) Continue taking your simvistatin 20mg daily.

You were admitted for a trans catheter aortic valve repair 
(TAVR)to treat your aortic valve stenosis which was done on 
___. By repairing the valve your heart can pump blood more

Structural Heart Team

It is very important to take all of your heart healthy 
medications. In particular, you are now taking aspirin and 
plavix. These medications help to prevent blood clots from 
forming on the new valve. If you stop these medications or miss 
___ dose, you risk causing a blood clot forming on your new valve. 
This could cause it to malfunction and it may be life 
threatening. Please do not stop taking aspirin or Plavix without 
taking to your heart doctor, even if another doctor tells you to 
stop the medications.  

You will need prophylactic antibiotics prior to any dental 
procedure. Please inform your dentist about your recent cardiac 
procedure, and obtain a prescription from your doctor before any 
procedure. 

Please weigh yourself every day in the morning after you go to 
the bathroom and before you get dressed. If your weight goes up 
by more than 3 lbs in 1 day or more than 5 lbs in 3 days, please 
call your heart doctor or your primary care doctor and alert 
them to this change. Your weight at discharge is 147.7 lbs.
 
We have made changes to your medication list, so please make 
sure to take your medications as directed. You will also need to 
have close follow up with your heart doctor and your primary 
care doctor.

recovery from your procedure or are experiencing any symptoms

to the hospital, please call the ___ HeartLine at ___ 
to speak to a cardiologist or cardiac nurse practitioner.  

It has been a pleasure to have participated in your care and we 
wish you the best with your health!

Brief Hospital Course:
Patient is an ___ male with a past medical history of a 
liver transplant in ___ due to alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, 
hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, CKD stage III, 
and recent stroke who presented with abdominal pain and bright 
red blood per rectum. 

# BRBPR # Abdominal pain  # Infrarenal aneurysm # Leukocytosis
Patient developed above mentioned pattern of BRBPR and presented 
first to an OSH, then to ___ for evaluation. He was also 
transferred to this hospital for vascular surgery evaluation in 
the setting of an infrarenal AAA. Vascular surgery thought there 
was no acute surgical need and he was admitted to medicine for 
his above symptoms. He had a significant leukocytosis which 
improved with conservative treatment. He was initially given 
Cipro and Flagyl in the emergency department but this was 
stopped on admission to the floor. Stool cultures were negative 
for Campylobacter, vibrio, Yersinia, E. coli, Salmonella, and 
Shigella. C. difficile testing was negative. The patient was 
managed conservatively and his symptoms resolved and he was able 
to tolerate eating and drinking prior to discharge. We think 
this was most likely an infectious colitis or possibly ischemia 
from peripheral arterial disease, though his outside imaging was 
reviewed with radiology and there were no suspicious findings of 
inflammation around watershed areas of the colon (no overt 
findings suggestive of ischemic colitis). His BRBPR resolved 
prior to discharge and he was sent home with PCP ___ 
recommended.

responded well to IVF administration.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. ___ is a ___ yo M who was riding a bike and stuck a 
motor vehicle head on a low speed. Reports positive loss of 
consciousness. CT head, c-spine, and chest that showed sternal 
fracture, left sided rib fractures. Xray of left shoulder 
negative for injury. The patient was admitted to the trauma 
floor for further observation and occupational therapy 
evaluation. 

Orthopedic surgery was consulted for left shoulder pain with 
decreased range of motion and pain. Follow up  in outpatient 
clinic was arranged.

Pain was well controlled on oral medication He tolerated a 
regular diet without issues. During this hospitalization, the 
patient ambulated early and frequently, was adherent with 
respiratory toilet and incentive spirometry, and actively 
participated in the plan of care. The patient received 
subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during this 
stay.

The patient was evaluated by occupational therapy due to patient 
report of loss of consciousness who cleared him for discharge to 
home. 

At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular 
diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well 
controlled.  The patient was discharged home without services. 
The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up 
instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with 
the discharge plan.
 
Medications on Admission:
None
 
Discharge Medications:
1.  Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q8H
